issue_title: simulator, pauses are not respected /not precise
issue_body:
## Expected Behavior
Using a pause of 4ms should add a pause of 4ms  
Using a pause of 27ms should add a pause of 27ms  

## Actual Behavior
Using a pause of 4ms is not visible in the sent audio  
Using a pause of 27ms adds a pause of 10ms (not always, sometimes it's not visible)  
I guess that you are using some syscall sleep to wait (low precision+kernel scheduler delays), while in the generator the pauses are made by soundcard samples (more precise).  
on windows sleep and GetTickCount has 1ms precision (more or less), to get higher precision there is QueryPerformanceCounter.  
But is better to just send many zero samples on the soundcard, unless maybe the precision is not sensitive (delay > 5 seconds?)  
**My use case:** i have this thermometer signal where data is repeated three times and i'd like to send all possible values to check what is actually supported (kind of fuzzing).  
but i can't use generator fuzzer since each transmission is repeated three times.  
**Bonus:**  auto-add delays in simulator like it happens in generator  

## Steps to Reproduce the Problem
Add a pause and record the signal using audacity

## Platform Specifications
msi, latest version

issue_title: Indicators which have two inputs with the same name are unusable
issue_body: Many indicators (e.g. `ti.add`) have two inputs both named `'real'`, which renders them unusable by the current bindings (unless, of course, you want to use the same data for both inputs).

1. List order is not kept when list is flattened and then returned as a dictionary with *as_dict()* if the length of the list is more than 10.
For example:
  ```
  In [1]: values = {'foo': list(range(11))}
  In [2]: flatdict.FlatDict(values).as_dict()
  Out [2]: {'foo': [0, 1, 10, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]}
  ```
2. A list of tuples (or lists) is not restored by *as_dict()*.
For example:
  ```
  In [1]: values = {'foo': [(1, 2, 3), (10, 20, 30)]}
  In [2]: flatdict.FlatDict(values).as_dict()
  Out [2]: {'foo': [1, 2, 3, 10, 20, 30]}
  ```
The diff attach file  [diff-flatdict.txt](https://github.com/gmr/flatdict/files/949787/diff-flatdict.txt) proposes code changes for these issues and an enhancement to control if lists and tuples are flattened (parameter *dict_only=False*, default value) or not (*dict_only=True*). For example:
  ```
  In [1]: values = {'foo': [(1, 2, 3), (10, 20, 30)],
                    'bar': {'baz': 4, 'qux': 5}}
  In [2]: flatdict_new.FlatDict(values, dict_only=True)
  Out [2]: {'bar:baz': 4, 'foo': [(1, 2, 3), (10, 20, 30)], 'bar:qux': 5}
  ```

issue_title: Select units in formatting - W vs VA?
issue_body:
In the default unit definitions, power is defined as follows:
[power] = [energy] / [time]
watt = joule / second = W = volt_ampere = VA

In electrical calculations, W and VA may be interchangeable from a units perspective, but when outputting results, a quantity usually is accurately represented with either W, VA, or var depending on the context. Is there any way to select which of the units get output when formatting?

(This is an extension of #14 and #259)

So currently, if you do

```python
f1 = Faker()
f2 = Faker()
f1.seed(123)
```

the `f2` object will effectively be seeded with `123` too due to all of the generators sharing the same `faker.generator.random` `Random()` instance, which is not really optimal.

I would be happy to do the work to fix this (by making the `random` object actually per-`Generator`), if you guys think it's a good idea.

```
~$ curl -version
curl: no URL specified!
curl: try 'curl --help' or 'curl --manual' for more information
~$ fuck
No fucks given
~$ curl --v http://www.google.com
curl: option --v: is ambiguous
curl: try 'curl --help' or 'curl --manual' for more information
~$ fuck
No fucks given

Even after typing a command correctly, like `curl --version`, thefuck is still not able to correct the typo `curl -version` (this is most likely because bash does not write the .bash_history file until the terminal has been closed).

<bunch of unimportant curl version information>
~$ curl -version
curl: no URL specified!
curl: try 'curl --help' or 'curl --manual' for more information
~$ fuck
No fucks given

Could thefuck go through the history of the ongoing bash session as well, before it is written to the history file? The entire history (including the ongoing session) can be accessed with the `history` command.

```
~$ history
    1  curl -version
    2  fuck
    3  curl --v http://www.google.com
    4  fuck
    5  curl --version
    6  curl -version
    7  fuck
    8  history

issue_title: pyspider webui: startup message "Web server running on port 25555" is misleading
issue_body:
```
$ pyspider
Web server running on port 25555
[I 170212 17:51:06 result_worker:49] result_worker starting...
[I 170212 17:51:07 tornado_fetcher:508] fetcher starting...
/home/miki/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyspider/webui/index.py:11: ExtDeprecationWarning: Importing flask.ext.login is deprecated, use flask_login instead.
  from flask.ext import login
[I 170212 17:51:07 processor:208] processor starting...
[I 170212 17:51:07 scheduler:569] scheduler starting...
[I 170212 17:51:07 app:76] webui running on 0.0.0.0:5000
[..snip...]
```

Many users, including me, will assume that this message indicates the port to specify in the browser url, e.g.: `http://localhost:25555`. Additional evidence: [a Google search for `web server running on port` (in quotes)](https://www.google.com/search?q=%22web+server+running+on+port%22).

From issue #309 I learned that this is the XMLRPC port, not the correct port for the browser URL. The relevant information is hidden somewhere down the log output: `webui running on 0.0.0.0:5000`.

Please consider making the initial startup message a bit more descriptive. Thank you.

issue_title: Error on keyring password get/set shouldn't disrupt user action
issue_body:
Currently, if an error occurs on password setting or retrieval that causes an exception to be raised, the user action is interrupted.

This is impractical; if there's anything strange with the keyring, the user should be allowed to just go on working by manually entering the password, and shouldn't need to disable the keyring extensions at all in order to proceed.

Also fixes a condition in the current testcases where the test would randomly work because it did not really set a keyring before being run - i.e. the test success was dependent on the order the tests were run. I had to fix this in order to add a unit test for my enhancement.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/32
- Originally reported by: Alan Franzoni
- Originally created at: 2010-10-27T14:39:11.577

issue_title: Mesh Intersection using Blender fails when the Blender default scene has been modified
issue_body:
Hi,

There is a bug in the mesh intersection (trimesh.boolean.intersect). While everything works well out of the box, the mesh intersection fails with a ValueError exception when I modify the default scene in Blender (I removed the standard cube which shows when starting up Blender). This is very unexpected behaviour and was difficult to track down. I guess the error also affects other operations which use the `blender.py.template` script.

The problem seems to come from the line 17 in `blender.py.template:`
` bpy.ops.object.mode_set(mode='OBJECT')`

This line fails with an error if no geometric object is present in the scene. More precisely, it seems to be related how Blender handles deletion of objects. I.e. it fails when I delete the default cube and try to run this line right after. However, when I actively select and deselect e.g. the camera, it works. So this might be a Blender bug, but still it would be good if trimesh would have some workaround :)


Thanks,
Delio

As a precursor to my question: I'm not here to cast doubt upon the usefulness of this project or the adequacy of those who have worked on it. In truth I am very impressed by this project and when I first discovered I began using it in most of my projects! (I do lots of data mining, so a library such as this is priceless.)

```
from pydash import filter_

In [.]: x = [{'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}, {'a': 2, 'b': 3, 'c': 4}]
In [.]: %timeit filter_(x, {'a': 1})
10000 loops, best of 3: 62.1 µs per loop

issue_title: composible listener API for circuit + stream events
issue_body:
Currently, the only way to listen for any of the circuit or stream events is by implementing the ``ICircuitListener`` or ``IStreamListener`` APIs (made slightly easier with corresponding ``CircuitListenerMixin`` etc). However, it would be nice to provide an API you can use without **having** to subclass anything.

That is, given a ``TorState`` instance right now you call ``.add_circuit_listener`` but augmenting this with, e.g., an API like ``.on_circuit_event('create', callable)`` might be nice (where the first argument is the "event name", e.g. one of "new", "launched", "extend", "built", "closed" or "failed" and the second arg is a callable that takes args depending on the event (always ``circuit`` arg, and then some number of kwargs, possibly none). Thus, a future-proof signature for the method would be ``def callback(circuit, **kwargs)``.

Ditto all that for the stream events, with slightly different details ;)

This would allow code like:

```
@inlineCallbacks
def main(reactor):
    proto = yield txtorcon.connect(UNIXClientEndpoint('/var/run/tor/control'))
    state = yield txtorcon.TorState.from_protocol(proto)

    def circuit_created(circ, **kw):
        print("new circuit", circ)
    yield state.on_circuit_event('new', circuit_created)
    # ...
```

autopep8 tries to convert "raise a, b, c" into "raise a(b, c)", but that's incorrect.  "raise a(b), None, c" would be correct in some cases but not all (depending on the values of a and b).  I don't think there's a guaranteed-safe way to make this transformation statically, so it's better to leave three-argument raise alone.

https://github.com/facebook/tornado/blob/master/tornado/web.py#L977

issue_title: Suggestion: keep sys.path per Evaluator instance
issue_body:
Currently Jedi uses sys.path directly and so editors have to modify sys.path in order to tell where modules locate.  This is not ideal.  For example, users may want to add Python 3 modules to sys.path while running Jedi in Python 2 (e.g., to write Python 2/3 compatible module).  To do that, I suggest to keep additional paths in a place other than sys.path.  Not only that, we can do more.  If we keep sys.path per Evaluator instance, then we can ask Jedi "what kind of modules I'd have if I have this set of `sys.path`?" without running Jedi in another Python process.  This may be useful, for example if you want to have different set of completions when you switch project.

(BTW, I am using partially 8 months-old knowledge of Jedi so, sorry if I miss some new features.)

issue_body:
Whenever running `wal -s`, it says it generated a new colorscheme, resets wallpaper, and "exports all files", but fails to rewrite the `sequences` file. That file remains unchanged, even though other files like `colors.json` show the correct new colors and wallpaper file.

Tried:  
* `wal -s -i wallpaper.jpg`
* `wal -ns -i wallpaper.jpg`
* `wal -n -s -i wallpaper.jpg`

`pywal` version: 1.3.3

The description in the ``setup.py`` starts with  bytes f09f 8ea8, but you don't have an encoding set for that  file. 

So anyones browser/editor will display that incorrectly unless they happen to have the same settings as the  ones used by you. In this case I see a white box placeholder on PyPi and when doing `pip show pywal` after installing pywal in a virtualbox

ERROR: start symbol translation_unit_or_empty undefined
```

The recent move to use `__slots__` in `c_ast.py` has broken a fair bit of my code. I pickle ASTs with arbitrary extended attributes, and both of these features now fail. Yes, I can work around them. However, in my opinion, the power of pycparser over something faster and more complete like libclang is the flexibility of Python, which `__slots__` undermines. (If speed is required, pypy executes pycparser about 3 times faster than CPython in my testing.) I think `__slots__` should be optional, if possible.

I forked and attempted to do this but this is hard to do in a way which has minimal impact on your code. I'd be willing to help, but I wanted to speak to you before working on a massive PR which you would understandably reject.

Currently my solution is a nasty monkey patch which I run before using pycparser which iterates through all the classes in c_ast.py, replacing them with clone classes with the same `__dict__` as the original but with the `__slots__` and member attributes removed to restore original behaviour.

Link to Reddit discussion for reference - http://www.reddit.com/r/Python/comments/38alfw/trying_to_define_a_class_which_can_optionally_use/

issue_title: mapping predict_proba columns to class labels
issue_body:
When building a model (e.g. SuperLearner) with `proba=True` on the meta estimator, how can I access the class labels for the `model.predict()` output columns.

sklearn classifiers generally expose a `classes_` property with the class labels, however this doesn't appear to be available on the mlens ensemble classes.

What is the preferred way to map the prediction output columns to class labels?

              , ('linear', LinearRegression())
              , ('knn', KNeighborsRegressor())

              , ('cat_boost', CatBoostRegressor(loss_function='MAE'))
            ]

params = {
    
      'random_forest':{'max_depth':max_depth_dist, 'n_estimators': n_est}

    , 'lgbm':{'max_depth':max_depth_dist, 'n_estimators': n_est}
    , 'cat_boost': {'max_depth':max_depth_dist, 'n_estimators': n_est}

}

from mlens.metrics import make_scorer
mae_scorer = make_scorer(mean_absolute_error, greater_is_better=False)

evaluator = Evaluator(mae_scorer, cv=5, random_state=seed, verbose=1)

issue_title: What is the actual meaning of busy time?
issue_body:
I am running a high disk IO test on linux, using psutil to  monitoring the linux disk usage. And I noticed that the busy time, which defined in document as " time spent doing actual I/Os (in milliseconds)", can be over 1000 milliseconds for each seconds. 

Could someone please tell me what is the actual meaning of busy time? 

here is my code:

```

issue_title: Use remote_tunnel only for local Docker registries
issue_body:
In situations when using custom registry with `PullDockerTasks`, `remote_tunnel` can take port which already used by some of containers. Therefore, `PullDockerTasks` should detect if `remote_tunnel` is really necessary.

In most cases `remote_tunnel` is necessary only for registries which point to the local host.

issue_title: No error returned, but process not completing when calling replace_roles.
issue_body:
Hello, I'm in the process of building a mute feature for a bot.  I'm using the API available through pip on python3.6, for clarity.

I have the problem narrowed down to the area in which I replace the roles.  The user exists, and so does the 'Muted' role, however, upon calling `replace_roles(member, *roles)`, no error is thrown, but the roles are not replaced.  I have tried using `remove_roles` by iterating over the user's roles, but this did not work either.  I saw in another thread in the issues that one is supposed to use replace instead of remove in this  instance.

The flow of logic is as follows:
```
if message.content.startswith("..mute"):
    # For getting the user's ID, as the command is used with a mention of the user
    to_mute = message.content.split(" ")[1][2:-1]
    member = discord.utils.find(lambda member: member.id == to_mute, message.channel.server.members)
    if member is not None:
        role = discord.utils.find(lambda role: role.name == 'Muted', message.channel.server.roles)
        if role is not None:
            client.replace_roles(member, role)
            tmp = yield from client.send_message(message.channel, "User muted!")
```

At which point, if I iterate over `member.roles`, it still retains all the old roles, despite printing that the user was muted.  This will likely prove to be an issue later when I am writing in a self-assignable roles feature.  Is there an piece of syntax I am missing?

issue_title: No event for if and only if a member was kicked from a server.
issue_body: You guys might be thinking ``You can get this in the on_member_remove`` event and check if a member was banned and if so return nothing. But in the event if a ``member`` is ``kicked`` or if the ``member`` left at their will it would mix the 2 up and log them as ``kicked`` even if they left at their will. So as such if you want to log raw ``kicks`` you cant right now sadly or easily. I am requesting for a event ``on_member_kicked`` to be created. so that way it can help resolve this issue.

issue_title: Please implement a flag to specify location of LibreOffice. "Cannot find a suitable office installation on your system."
issue_body:
Hey. Useful project.

I've managed to get LibreOffice & unoconv installed on Heroku with `heroku-buildpack-apt` but was disappointed to find that unoconv trips up on the first hurdle.

`heroku-buildpack-apt` installs LibreOffice here:
```
~ $ which libreoffice
/app/.apt/usr/bin/libreoffice
```

And surprisingly

```
~ $ unoconv
unoconv: Cannot find a suitable office installation on your system.
ERROR: Please locate your office installation and send your feedback to:
       http://github.com/dagwieers/unoconv/issues
~ $ unoconv --help
unoconv: Cannot find a suitable office installation on your system.
ERROR: Please locate your office installation and send your feedback to:
       http://github.com/dagwieers/unoconv/issues
```

That's a REALLY unhelpful error.

Countless issues have been raised (#263, #177, #165, #122) just because the path can't be found.

I sort of expected a flag that I could explicitly tell unoconv where my custom location of `LibreOffice` is: something like `unoconv --libreoffice-path /app/.apt/usr/bin/libreoffice`.

Then at least I can get on with my work without being forced to write an issue here on GitHub.

Thanks.

issue_title: On Windows --export-statsd  terminates immediately and does not export.
issue_body:
#### Description
- When executing --export-statsd on Windows; glances terminates immediately and does not export stats
- Based on the below; assuming this is an issue with the --export in general, as standalone mode cannot be started on Windows
- It appears this is a regression from 2.8.6 related to the fix of "Global name standalone not defined (#1030)" which reverted the fix from #1038 in __init__ to 
   if core.is_standalone()and not WINDOWS: from    if core.is_standalone():
- Commenting out "and not WINDOWS" causes the export to function as expected.

#### Versions

* Glances (glances -V): v2.8.8
* PSutil (glances -V): v5.2.0
* Operating System (lsb_release -a): Windows 7

#### Logs
This is the whole of the log:
2017-03-27 09:53:24,388 -- INFO -- Start Glances 2.8.8
2017-03-27 09:53:24,388 -- INFO -- CPython 2.7.8 and PSutil 5.2.0 detected
2017-03-27 09:53:24,403 -- INFO -- On Windows OS, export disable the Web interface

issue_body:
Problem. I have a table with different column widths, I set the widths as percentages in the <th> tags. if I don't use the uitheme then it works fine. If I do use the uithem (in this case bootstrap_3) then the columns are forced to be all the same width.

If I set the theme to bootstrap, e.g. 
   ```
  $.tablesorter.themes.bootstrap = {
      etc, etc
       iconSortAsc  : 'glyphicon glyphicon-chevron-up',
       iconSortDesc : 'glyphicon glyphicon-chevron-down', 
      }
   tablesorter(
    {
      theme : "bootstrap",
      headerTemplate : '{content} {icon}',
    widgets: ['uitheme', 'stickyHeaders', 'filter', 'zebra'],
   etc

```
then the column headers get up and down glyphicons but the columns are all the same size

If I set the theme to bootstrap but don't set 'uitheme' 

  ```
 tablesorter(
    {
      theme : "bootstrap",
      headerTemplate : '{content} {icon}',
    widgets: ['stickyHeaders', 'filter', 'zebra'],
   etc
```
   
then the column widths are as they should be, and I get some styling, it adds .tablesorter-bootstrap to the table, but I don't get the up and down glyphicons. I.e. it doesn't add those classes to the icon tag.

If I force the width of the columns by adding some css to force a fixed width to the filter inputs, then the columns do start wider. But only for a fraction of a second, then the 'uitheme' code takes over and forces the columns to be all equal sized.

issue_title: Documment use of Datasets without files
issue_body:
I'm not sure the code was intended for this, but it is a nice feature to have.
Bassicaly I'm using pydicom to parse and write out to dcm4chee pat_attrs colum directly to database, since it is a blob of a dicom file in the database (and only the parts referring to the patient), there is no real file to speak of.

Besides, reading into it as a BytesIO, I also had to open it as DicomFileLike, and manually set its, _implicit_VR to be able to write to it. I think this should be simpler, but we should document it as is if this is the current way to do it.

Here is the function I used to get a Dataset and change the PatientID:

``` python
import pydicom
from io import BytesIO

def fix_patient_dcm(blob, pid=None):
    dataset = pydicom.filereader.read_dataset(BytesIO(blob), False, True) # easy enough, but undocumented, docstring says first arg is filename as str
    if pid == None:
        pid = dataset.PatientID[:-1]
    dataset.PatientID = pid
    with BytesIO() as dcm: # seems overkill to have to open an BytesIO and then a DicomFileLike just to get the dataset as blob
        dcm = pydicom.filebase.DicomFileLike(dcm)
        dcm._implicit_VR = False # seems like a bug, will fail otherwise
        pydicom.filewriter.write_dataset(dcm, dataset)
        dcm.seek(0)
        newblob = dcm.read()
    return newblob
```

On file "filereader.py", line 720:
`logger.debug(u"Reading file '{0}'".format(fp))
`
Sometimes there is a problem:

> UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xc3 in position 15: ordinal not in range(128)


The code simply stops because it tries to decode an already decoded string. I removed the line but I'm in a hurry and was not able to debug and fix this. When I remove the line, since this is only a logger, everything works fine.

issue_title: Add support for using with a QueueHandler/QueueListener
issue_body:
If Python3's QueueHandler/QueueListener are used then record.exc_info is
None, but record.exc_text contains the traceback. Reading the source of
the logging module it would appear that exc_text can safely be assumed
to contain the exception text if it is not None, so can be used to populate
graylog's full_message.

#18 contains an attempt to fix this

issue_title: Modifiers in assignment block inside of test block in the xml don't get recognized.
issue_body:
When placing an assignment block inside of a test block the modifiers don't get assigned properly. For example if the this:
```
    	<assignment name="SymOps" value="symops" allocate="true">
  	  <value identifier="symops" file="symops.in.{}"
  		 folder="../tests/simple_cubic" rename="symops.in"/>
	</assignment>
```
 assignment tag appeared after the end of the test block then the modifiers are:
```
('modifiers', 'intent(in), allocatable')
```
However if the same variable gets the modifiers of:
```
('modifiers', 'intent(in))
```

These modifiers change how the data gets read into the variables, if only ```intent(in)``` is passed then the method ```py_read_f``` is used instead of ```py_read``` which handles the allocation properly. The modifiers are read from the parent attribute of the ```AssignmentValue``` class in the method ```_code_file``` on line 1384:
```
modifiers = self.parent.global_attr("modifiers", [])
```
 then used to determine which ```py_read``` to use. It would seem that when the xml is parsed to make it's global_attributes that the modifiers are being missed for some reason.

issue_title: mlogvis: remove write lock and read lock grouping
issue_body: Lock stats aren't reported in MongoDB 3.0+ and are not relevant for the WiredTiger storage engine. These groupings should be removed from `mlogvis`.

From https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/issues/1662: there is a TypeError when generating slides with hieroglyph under Sphinx 1.3b2.

The problem lies in the `slide_context` function returning the context object. In Sphinx 1.2, the `html-page-context` event had no return value, so the return value was ignored. In Sphinx 1.3, the return value has a meaning: it overrides the template name. But for that it must be a string, not a dictionary.

    INTERNALERROR> AttributeError: 'Config' object has no attribute 'cache'

Could `pytest-flake8`'s usage of the `cacheprovider` plugin be made optional?

issue_title: Reset color string, with use of "color"
issue_body:
In Version 1.6.1 there is a reset color sequnece (<esc>[0m) send to the terminal even if no color is used when an cli application terminates.
The reason is the line 
0017:    atexit.register(_reset)
in module colors.py.
While this might not hurt most of the time, it looks funny on Windows machines, where the sequence is not handled correctly out of the box.
Obviously the call of _reset() is very usefull to reset the terminal if any color-functions are used. But a better way is needed to decide when it should be called.

Rüdiger
This may allow us to completely remove the mask from TF actor-critics. This is obviously a big breaking change.

issue_title: Make it work on Raspberry Pi
issue_body:
See the commands to get the Hz:

```bash
cat /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/cpufreq/cpuinfo_max_freq
cat /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/cpufreq/cpuinfo_min_freq
cat /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/cpufreq/cpuinfo_cur_freq
```

Currently the setup.py includes a requirements.txt, which requires specific versions of every requirement. This causes problems when combined with other software since they could require a version range which isn't compatible with the specific version required here.

Or consider another scenario: I need a newer version of e.g. requests, then I'm stuck because this package is pinned to an older version.

This article goes into a bit more depth about why setup.py should be treated differently to requirements.txt https://caremad.io/posts/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/

My suggestion is that the dependencies should be moved into setup.py and the version should be changed to minimum versions (using >=) rather than exact version requirements.

## Example script that uses authentication
```
#!/usr/bin/python

import requests
import json
import sys
import os

drill_base_url = "https://drillbit.local:8047"

drill_user = "username"
drill_pass = "password"

print "Drill Rest Endpoint: %s" % (drill_base_url)

def main():
    s = requests.Session() # Create a session object
    s = authDrill(s)        # Authenticate to Drill

    r = runQuery(s, "ALTER SESSION set `store.json.all_text_mode` = true")
    result = runQuery(s, "show files")

    print r.text
    
    if result.status_code == 200:
        print result.text
    else:
        print "Error encountered: %s" % result.status_code

def runQuery(s, drill):
    url = drill_base_url + "/query.json"   
    payload = {"queryType":"SQL", "query":drill}
    headers = {"Content-type": "application/json"}
    r = s.post(url, data=json.dumps(payload), headers=headers, verify=False)

    return r
def authDrill(s):

    url = drill_base_url + "/j_security_check"

    login = {'j_username': drill_user, 'j_password': drill_pass}
   
    r = s.post(url, data=login, verify=False)

    if r.status_code == 200:
        if r.text.find("Invalid username/password credentials") >= 0:
            print "Authentication Failed - Please check Secrets - Exiting"
            sys.exit(1)
        elif r.text.find("Number of Drill Bits") >= 0:
            print "Authentication successful"
        else:
            print "Unknown Response Code 200 - Exiting"
            print r.text
            sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print "Non HTTP-200 returned - Unknown Error - Exiting"
        print "HTTP Code: %s" % r.status_code
        print r.text
        sys.exit(1)

    return s

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()




```

The next fire time of a CronTrigger is wrong if it crosses a dst switch.

Daily trigger: 8 o'clock CE(S)T
But today, the first day with dst this year, the next fire time returned was 9 o'clock CEST.

Here's some code to replicate in ipython
```
#!python
import pytz
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from apscheduler.triggers.cron import CronTrigger
cet = pytz.timezone('CET')
trigger = CronTrigger(hour=8, timezone=cet)  # once every day at 8 CE(S)T

datetime_cet = cet.localize(datetime(2016, 3, 26, 21))
correct_next_date = cet.localize(datetime(2016, 3, 27, 8))
print 'expected', correct_next_date, '# used cet'
print 'but got ', trigger.get_next_fire_time(None, datetime_cet), '# used cet'

# now in europe/berlin
cet = pytz.timezone('Europe/Berlin')
trigger = CronTrigger(hour=8, timezone=cet)  # once every day at 8 CE(S)T

datetime_cet = cet.localize(datetime(2016, 3, 26, 21))
correct_next_date = cet.localize(datetime(2016, 3, 27, 8))
print 'expected', correct_next_date, '# used europe/berlin'
print 'but got ', trigger.get_next_fire_time(None, datetime_cet), '# used europe/berlin'

# now with a start_date already in the correct dst setting
datetime_cet = cet.localize(datetime(2016, 3, 27, 7))
correct_next_date = cet.localize(datetime(2016, 3, 27, 8))
print 'expected', correct_next_date, '# used europe/berlin after dst change'
print 'and got ', trigger.get_next_fire_time(None, datetime_cet), '# used europe/berlin after dst change'
print 'so the trigger time differs w.r.t. the input timezone. this is unfortunate'

# now some experimenting
print
last_fire_time = cet.localize(datetime(2016, 3, 26, 8))
print last_fire_time, '+ 1 day is', last_fire_time + timedelta(days=1), '# no dst change!'
print last_fire_time, '+ 1 day normalized is', cet.normalize(last_fire_time + timedelta(days=1)), '# bad'
print last_fire_time, 'as naive then + 1 day then to cet is', cet.localize(last_fire_time.replace(tzinfo=None) + timedelta(days=1)), '# that is what I want!'
```
And the output:

```
expected 2016-03-27 08:00:00+02:00 # used cet
but got  2016-03-27 09:00:00+02:00 # used cet
expected 2016-03-27 08:00:00+02:00 # used europe/berlin
but got  2016-03-27 09:00:00+02:00 # used europe/berlin
expected 2016-03-27 08:00:00+02:00 # used europe/berlin after dst change
and got  2016-03-27 08:00:00+02:00 # used europe/berlin after dst change
so the trigger time differs w.r.t. the input timezone. this is unfortunate

2016-03-26 08:00:00+01:00 + 1 day is 2016-03-27 08:00:00+01:00 # no dst change!
2016-03-26 08:00:00+01:00 + 1 day normalized is 2016-03-27 09:00:00+02:00 # bad
2016-03-26 08:00:00+01:00 as naive then + 1 day then to cet is 2016-03-27 08:00:00+02:00 # that is what I want!
```


If I replace lines 117, 118 in _set_field_value of apscheduler.trigger.cron.CronTrigger
```
#!python
        difference = datetime(**values) - dateval.replace(tzinfo=None)
        return self.timezone.normalize(dateval + difference)
```
with
```
#!python
        return self.timezone.localize(datetime(**values))
```
it might do the trick -- but I do not yet unterstand why a difference was constructed in the first place.
I'm not sure if the last two lines in _increment_field_value also play a role in this issue.

issue_title: One-off job incorrectly scheduled if scheduler timezone different from system timezone
issue_body:
I have a scheduler running on UTC, but my system time is CDT (UTC-5). When I attempt to run a one-off job, `DateTrigger` calls `convert_to_datetime()` and applies the UTC zone to the naive output of `datetime.now()`, which results in the job being scheduled five hours in the past.

Presumably if you're in a timezone ahead of UTC, the job will be scheduled in the future, rather than immediately.

I have a nagging feeling I must be missing something obvious, but I don't see what.

Code to reproduce (assuming your local time is not UTC):

```
from pytz import utc
from apscheduler.schedulers.background import BlockingScheduler
import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

def hello():
    logging.info("Hello from scheduled job.")

scheduler = BlockingScheduler(timezone=utc)
scheduler.add_job(hello)
scheduler.start()
```

My results (running at 2016-04-28 08:29:42 CDT):

```
INFO:apscheduler.scheduler:Adding job tentatively -- it will be properly scheduled when the scheduler starts
INFO:apscheduler.scheduler:Added job "hello" to job store "default"
INFO:apscheduler.scheduler:Scheduler started
DEBUG:apscheduler.scheduler:Looking for jobs to run
WARNING:apscheduler.executors.default:Run time of job "hello (trigger: date[2016-04-28 08:29:42 UTC], next run at: 2016-04-28 08:29:42 UTC)" was missed by 5:00:00.000989
INFO:apscheduler.scheduler:Removed job bb7b6db0f4da4386a0e65962cdcdb974
DEBUG:apscheduler.scheduler:No jobs; waiting until a job is added
```

Not tested, but I suppose the fix would be something along the lines of this (in `apscheduler/triggers/date.py`):
```
    def __init__(self, run_date=None, timezone=None):
        if run_date:
            timezone = astimezone(timezone) or get_localzone()
        else:
            timezone = get_localzone()
        self.run_date = convert_to_datetime(run_date or datetime.now(), timezone, 'run_date')
```

issue_title: snap packaging is self-reporting the wrong version string
issue_body:
I started playing with the "snappy" packaging yesterday (and I have to say it's pretty cool.. I had no idea how it worked before, the namespace-based isolation is pretty slick). But I noticed that `wormhole --version` reports the wrong thing:

```
warner@snaptest2:~$ sudo snap install wormhole
...
warner@snaptest2:~$ wormhole --version
magic-wormhole 0.10.2+0.g13ff246.dirty
```

magic-wormhole uses [Versioneer](https://github.com/warner/python-versioneer) to manage the version string, which makes the setup.py run `git describe` to figure out what's in the source tree, and processes that to determine the version string. When setup.py is asked to make a build artifact (like `setup.py sdist` or `setup.py wheel`), it embeds this generated string into one of the source files (`src/wormhole/_version.py`) so all non-git-checkout source trees derived from that artifact will get the right version.

I'm guessing the snap build process is modifying one of the non-ignored source files, and then `setup.py` is invoked, so it thinks the tree is dirty, and creates a version string with that `+0.g13ff246.dirty` suffix.

One way to fix this might be to have the snap build process start by making an sdist or a wheel (`python setup.py sdist`), and then install from that. The filename will be somewhat unpredictable, but maybe something like `pip install dist/magic-wormhole*` would work, assuming it's run in a clean checkout with no other .tar.gz files in `dist/` already. Or maybe there's a way to tell `setup.py sdist` to write the output to a specific filename.

Or, if we could figure out what's being modified, maybe there's a way to make it stop.

@elopio , what do you think? I've only just started looking at the snapcraft documentation, so I'm not sure what's the best way to address this one.

It would be better if you could be specific about the reason over "OVER_QUERY_LIMIT" errors , as 
1) The number of requests is unclear
2) The same error is returned for some legitimate place names as well. After the error , I am able to access details of another place in the very next request (though it says "OVER_QUERY_LIMIT").
( It is not covering all places .)

from decimal import Decimal

class MyModel(Model):
    name: str
    value: Decimal
    user: str

    def clean_value(self, value):
        return sanitize_decimal(value)
```

And I need a sanitized value because my value contains a comma. So I need to create an object like this:

```
my_object = MyModel(name='Rafael Henrique', amount='23232,43', user='rafaelhenrique')
```

But when I call clean this problem happens below:

```
>>> my_object = MyModel(name='Rafael Henrique', amount='23232,43', user='rafaelhenrique')

Traceback (most recent call last):

decimal.InvalidOperation: [<class 'decimal.ConversionSyntax'>]
```

To "solve" this problem i need use this method (below) to override clean:

```
    def clean(self):
        self.value = self.clean_value(self.value)

My suggestion: pysimplemodel read the clean_<field> first and then call clean from the base class after.

This is a really great project and I am interested in using it during development of extension modules. I would however prefer to use the more traditional methods of building extensions for releases in a lot of cases. The examples of configuration shown in the README are awkward in the sense that they use raw mako templates in the .cpp files directly which would result in failed builds if compiled directly. Some possible solutions:

- Put the mako configuration in comments (I tested this and it works without issue)
- Explicitly note in the README that the above is acceptable
- Optionally take a keyword argument in `cppimport.imp` for configuration and bypass mako entirely

issue_title: Allow ETA, AbsoluteETA, etc to change the string prefixes
issue_body: `AbsoluteETA` currently prepends a long string "Estimated finish time: " before the date.  The base `Timer` object allows the `format` kwarg to modify the text printed.  `AbsoluteETA` and `ETA` should allow something similar, rather than hardcode the prefix string(s).

For many slow-moving loops, the transfer speed would be something like .01 B/s or .00 B/s, which is highly uninformative. Is there a way you could show s/B for these cases? 175 s/B is much more descriptive and helpful. Also, do you have a workaround I could use for now? Because seeing .00 B/s doesn't tell me much about how fast I'm looping.

issue_title: Merge working tests MR and ask people to rebase
issue_body:
If #232 is merged we have a working test-suite and after that we can go through the rest of the MR and rebase against new working tests.

There are also a lot of duplicated fixes for some of the errors and the list of MR will be more slim after that merge.
`self.root_enabler.disable` should be called on makereport rather than teardown.
`SubfieldBase` is used in `fields.py`. It's been deprecated and will be removed in Django 1.10.

I am able to save an attachment to an email most of the time, but on occasion it "hangs".  If I let it keep running, over time I will see on the exchange server side the the same file is being attached to the email multiple times.  The way I am adding the attachment and saving is:

```python
def attatchfile(email,filename,path,logger):
    logger.info("Begin attatching %s", filename)
    filename_and_path = path + filename
    logger.info("File full path is %s", filename_and_path)
    logger.info("Open file for reading as binary")
    with open(filename_and_path, mode='rb') as file:  # b is important -> binary
        logger.info("Read file into memory")
        binary_file_content = file.read()
    logger.info("Create file object to use as attatchment")
    my_file = FileAttachment(name=filename, content=binary_file_content)
    logger.info("Save file onto email object")
    email.attach(my_file)
    logger.info("Save email object back to exchange")
    email.save()
    logger.info("Done saving email object back to exchange")
    return "Successful"
```

The email.save() is never returning and that is where duplicate attachments are getting put onto the. email.

Possible Solution:  If this is due to some type of issue with the communication with the exchange server, then should I handle this with threading?  I can spin off a thread that calls this attachment function and kill it if it doesn't return in a set time.  However, if I do that, I would want to revert the email back to it's original state before I attached anything.  Is there a way to have a second, untouched email object open that I can re-save at this point to undo any changes that I may have already pushed to the server or would I need to keep track of the changes and undo them?

I have a StackOverflow [question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/38032782/why-is-my-logstash-handler-throwing-a-typeerror), but it hasn't gotten any attention, so I thought I'd bring it up here.

___

# The requirement:

Create a logger that can log the `hostname` as a part of the formatter. The logger will have many handlers, one of which is a logstash handler.

# The code:

```
import logging
import logstash

from socket import gethostname

class CustomLogger(logging.Logger):
    def _log(self, level, msg, args, exc_info=None, extra=None):
        if extra is None:
            extra = { 'hostname' : gethostname() }
        super(CustomLogger, self)._log(level, msg, args, exc_info, extra)

def setup_custom_logger(host, port):
    # add hostname to the formatter.
    logging.setLoggerClass(CustomLogger)

    formatter = logging.Formatter(fmt='%(hostname)s - %(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(module)s - %(message)s')

    logstash_handler = logstash.LogstashHandler(host, port, version=2)
    logstash_handler.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
    logstash_handler.setFormatter(formatter)

    logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
    logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
    logger.addHandler(logstash_handler)

    return logger
```

Currently, the package does not have a way to implement constraints into its optimization process.  For example, one cannot [exclude points](https://github.com/fmfn/BayesianOptimization/issues/39).  The linked discussion suggests that generalizing the exclusion problem to formal constraints is the better way to go.
 
After some investigation, I've found that the implementation will be pretty easy.  Since the ``acq_max`` function uses SciPy's ``minimize`` function, it's simply a matter of allowing the user to optionally include constraints in the instantiation method and passing those constraints into ``minimize`` through ``acq_max``.  We'll also have to switch the optimization method from L-BFGS-B to SLSQP when the user passes constraints.
 
The only question I have about the switch is whether to give the user an option to pass the Jacobian of constraint functions.  ``minimize`` has an option for that, but it only speeds the optimization up in [some cases](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35826828/should-slsqp-minimization-speed-up-with-an-analytic-jacobian).  Perhaps it's unnecessary.
 
I'll do this ASAP.

issue_title: Sub Periods don't get their parent's timezone
issue_body:
I have to say this one was a doozy, there are just too many places where dates are timezone converted. These might be related to a number of other issues.

## TL;DR

``Period`` needs to include its timezone info when requested to generate sub Periods, otherwise some recurrences rules fail because a recurrence parameters (like week day number) in a given datetime in UTC might be different from other timezones.

## Long explanation

Django stores ``start`` and ``end`` date fields of ``Event`` model in UTC, but there is no place where the original timezone is stored, for this reason the ``get_occurrences`` method (and auxiliary methods) in ``Event`` model takes the tzinfo from the start date of the parameter and localizes the dates from the models with the timezone on every request.

``Period`` class represents a timespan and is in charge of returning the occurrences that happen inside it. ``Period`` also can generate sub periods of time upon request (instances of Period). This is used when building the slots in the calendar web UI. Internally, the Period class uses normalized timezone-awared dates in UTC, and the ``tzinfo`` parameter is used to normalize and localize dates before sending them to ``Event.get_occurrences``.

### The problem
All generated sub periods are missing its parent tzinfo, so it defaults to ``UTC``. 

### Symptoms
Occurrences are generated in incorrect dates.

### Example to illustrate

* Config django to be in a timezone != UTC, in my case ``UTC-3`` ("America/Montevideo").
* Create a ``Daily`` rule that only occurs on Saturdays (``byweekno:SA`` or ``byweekno:5``)
* Create an event that starts on a Saturday at 22:00 hs, example ``Sat 2017-01-07 22:00 UYT``
* Event will be stored in model and DB as ``Sun 2017-01-08 01:00`` (UTC). Notice that the hour is now 1am in UTC, and the rule is "every Saturday".
* Get occurrences for event between ``Sat 2017-01-06 22:00 UYT`` and ``Sat 2017-01-28 23:00 UYT``. It will return the correct days but all start times will be on 1 am UTC. Later on when these datetimes get converted to the user timezone, they'll be one day early.

Hey guys,

In my Django settings, I have L10N active and the timezone set to "Europe/Paris". 

When a recurring event has been set before DST started, the event time is shown accurately before the DST started. When DST has started, the event time changes to be one hour later. I know this might have something to do with timezone aware date times, but there must be an easy fix for that besides running my whole Django setup timezone naive. I'd prefer not to do that, as the project will span multiple time zones later.

Did I set up the server faulty or how do you tackle this issue? 

Thanks in advance.

issue_body:
I found an issue about the calculation of CLV. 

In customer_lifetime_value(), the discounted cash flow for each period is calculated as:
```
df['expected_revenues_period_'+str(i)] = df.apply(
    lambda r: (m*transaction_prediction_model.predict(i, r['frequency'], r['recency'], r['T'])/(1+d)**(i/30)),
    axis=1
    )
```

in which,
*i* is th looping counter (in steps of 30), 
*m* is self.conditional_expected_average_profit(),
*d* is discount rate.
*transaction_prediction_model.predict()* returns "conditional_expected_number_of_purchases_up_to_time"

It seems to me that *m* should be the expected profit for individual customer instead of all the training records, and the discounted cash flow for a single period is `m * (predict(t) - predict(t-1)) / (1+d)**(i/30)`

May I propose to make such change:
```
df['monetary_value'] = monetary_value

for i in range(30, (time*30)+1, 30):
    df['expected_revenues_period_'+str(i)] = df.apply(
          lambda r: (r['monetary_value']*
              (transaction_prediction_model.predict(i, r['frequency'], r['recency'], r['T'])
               - transaction_prediction_model.predict(i - 30, r['frequency'], r['recency'], r['T'])) /(1+d)**(i/30)),
              axis=1
     )
```

To integrate it, i naively used the basic sample from Readme: https://github.com/borntyping/python-colorlog#usage

So i coded something like that:

```
root_logger = colorlog.getLogger("boussole")
root_logger.setLevel(level)
root_logger.setFormatter(colorlog.ColoredFormatter(
    '%(log_color)s%(levelname)s:%(name)s:%(message)s'))
root_logger.addHandler(logging.StreamHandler())
```

And this resulted in exception complaining about "Logger" that didn't have the `setFormatter` because this is only a method available from a handler.

Stopping to be so naive, i simply reformatted my code to set formatter from my handler and it works well:

```
    root_logger = logging.getLogger("boussole")
    root_logger.setLevel(level)
    handler = logging.StreamHandler()
    handler.setFormatter(
        colorlog.ColoredFormatter(
            '%(asctime)s - %(log_color)s%(message)s',
            datefmt="%H:%M:%S"
        )
    )

    root_logger.addHandler(handler)
```

Did i miss something or maybe your sample is not very accurate ?

issue_title: swagger.json does not support custom URL converters
issue_body:
Similar issue to #233.

Minimal working example (to enable parsing the URL according to a regular expression):
```python
from flask import Flask
from werkzeug.routing import BaseConverter
from flask_restplus import Api, Resource


class RegexConverter(BaseConverter):
    def __init__(self, url_map, *items):
        super(RegexConverter, self).__init__(url_map)
        self.regex = items[0]


app = Flask(__name__)
app.url_map.converters['regex'] = RegexConverter
api = Api(app, version='1.0', title='RegexExample')


@api.route('/<regex("[0-9]*"):uid>/')
# @api.route('/<uid>/')
class Example(Resource):
    def get(self, uid):
        return {'success': uid}


if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True)
```

* With `@api.route('/<uid>/')` everything is obviously working:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2772505/23256381/7786129e-f9bf-11e6-9f98-e51efaa6748e.png)


* With `@api.route('/<regex("[0-9]*"):uid>/')` it all explodes:
```
  File "/home/user/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flask_restplus/swagger.py", line 82, in extract_path_params
    raise ValueError('Unsupported type converter: {}, {}'.format(descriptor, name))
ValueError: Unsupported type converter: regex("[0-9]*"), uid
```

issue_body:
The documentation in the [README](https://github.com/okunishinishi/python-stringcase/blob/cc7d5eb58ff4a959a508c3f2296459daf7c3a1f2/README.rst) and on pypi is out of sync with the implementation (1.2.0):

```
>>> stringcase.constcase('FooBarBaz') # => "_FOO_BAR_BAZ"
'FOO_BAR_BAZ'
>>> stringcase.pathcase('FooBarBaz') # => "/foo/bar/baz"
'foo/bar/baz'
>>> stringcase.snakecase('FooBarBaz') # => "_foo_bar_baz"
'foo_bar_baz'
>>> stringcase.spinalcase('FooBarBaz') # => "-foo-bar-baz"
'foo-bar-baz'
>>> stringcase.titlecase('FooBarBaz') # => " Foo Bar Baz"
'Foo Bar Baz'
>>> stringcase.alphanumcase('_Foo., Bar') # =>'FooBar'
'_FooBar'
```

For all except the last one, I think the code is doing the right thing and the docs are wrong.

I presume alphanumcase is meant to drop all non-alphanumeric characters, so the fact that it currently retains '_' seems problematic.

issue_title: whatArgs reports incorrect arg name for createTestCase
issue_body:
whatArgs('createTestCase')

reports the following:

"createTestCase(<testcasename>, <testsuiteid>, <testprojectid>, <authorlogin>, <summary>, [steps=<steps>], [precondition
s=<preconditions>], [importance=<importance>], [execution=<execution>], [order=<order>], [internalid=<internalid>], [che
ckduplicatedname=<checkduplicatedname>], [actiononduplicatedname=<actiononduplicatedname>], [devKey=<devKey>])\n createT
estCase: Create a test case\n        positional args: testcasename, testsuiteid, testprojectid, authorlogin,\n
               summary\n        optional args : preconditions, importance, execution, order, internalid,\n
          checkduplicatedname, actiononduplicatedname\n                        \n        argument 'steps' will be set wi
th values from .stepsList, \n        - when argsOptional does not include a 'steps' item\n        - .stepsList can be fi
lled before call via .initStep() and .appendStep()\n        "

In particular, it says that `[execution=<execution>]`

However, when using `createTestCase` with this argument (e.g. `execution=2`), the test case was not set to 'Automated', as expected, although no error was reported and the test case was successfully created.  

After some searching and testing, I found that I could use `executiontype` instead of `execution` and I would get the result I was expecting.  Note that `executiontype` is not reported by `whatArgs`.

So, it seems likely that 'whatArgs` is reporting `execution` when it should be reporting `executiontype`

issue_title: Running the parser with one alternative keyword provides much more accurate results than a list of keywords.
issue_body:
The first time I ran the parser I used the following. 

memes = findMemes("meme",Meme_secondary_keywords,i)

Where "findMemes" is simply the parser wrapped in a method, Meme_secondary_keywords is a list of meme related terms like "greg,trump, and kobe" and i is superfluous

The second time I ran the parser I switched to this method

for word in variety_keywords:
       Secondarykeyword = []
       Secondarykeyword.append(word)
       memes = findMemes(search_keyword,Secondarykeyword,i)
       i = i + 1

In the above, i just acts as a tag that is added onto each unique results folder.

The results from the second method contained FAR more "meme-like" content. Reviewing the results from the first method, I noticed that after the first 50 or so results I was getting very few memes. 

For example, the word "Trump" is the 11th element of the list, so it will run after 1,100 searches have been done. The first method of running the method did not return any trump memes, all of the results were just generic pictures of trump. The second method returned over 95 pictures which I would classify as memes

This is likely an idiosyncrasy of google's image search algorithm. I would hypothesize that as you get further from the original results page the scope of the results widens dramatically. I do not know if this problem can be fixed on your end, but I think it would be useful for end users to be aware of the second method. If I had ran my parse with the first method my results would be unusable, but the second method gave me very good data 

See the master class in my memeClassifier for an example of this behavior https://github.com/dkennedy778/memeClassifier

Searching for memes with the for loop provides very good results, but searching with just the method and keyword is practically useless

issue_title: Multiple workers with Redis don't invalidate cache
issue_body:
Hello,

After deploying Django with Daphne and Redis (asgi_redis) as in documentation, I made a Circus config to run 4 workers, Redis and Daphne. The problem is that using multiple workers, the cache is not invalidated at some times. For example I make a change in the app and start refreshing. Sometimes the change will appear, sometimes not. Also, the frontend part of the app is separate (a React app) and not using Django templates. I found no way to specify exactly when to invalidate the cache (after an action, not a number of seconds). And when I use only one worker I don't have this issue. Is this a bug or am I doing something wrong?

Versions:
asgi-redis==1.4.3
channels==1.1.8
daphne==1.4.2
Django==1.11.9

We get this in daphne error logs:

```2017-05-13 13:58:08,325 DEBUG    HTTP GET request for daphne.response.EdtdafmMUb!WqrtfODWix
2017-05-13 13:58:08,375 DEBUG    HTTP 404 response started for daphne.response.EdtdafmMUb!WqrtfODWix
2017-05-13 13:58:08,376 DEBUG    HTTP close for daphne.response.EdtdafmMUb!WqrtfODWix
2017-05-13 13:58:08,376 INFO     "127.0.0.1" - - [13/May/2017:13:58:07 +0000] "GET /ws/user/ HTTP/1.1" 404 82 "-" "-"
2017-05-13 13:58:08,377 DEBUG    HTTP response complete for daphne.response.EdtdafmMUb!WqrtfODWix
```

This is our `routing.py`:
```
    ...
    route('websocket.receive', ws_message_user, path=r'^/ws/user/'),
    route('websocket.receive', ws_message_driver, path=r'^/ws/driver/'),
    
    route('websocket.connect', ws_add_user, path=r'^/ws/user/'),
    route('websocket.connect', ws_add_driver, path=r'^/ws/driver/'),
    ...
```

This is supervisor conf:
```
[program:daphne_production]
environment=
        DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE="zxc.settings.production",
        TZ_LOG_DIR="/var/log/zxc-logs/production/django/",
        DB_USER="db_user",

stdout_logfile=/var/log/zxc/supervisor/daphne/daphne.log
```

We have a 8 core server with 19gb ram.

I added more Daphne instances which get the number of 404 responses from 33% to around 1% but again last day it spiked to 26%. Then again today it came down to around 9%.

My questions are:

1. Why do we get these 404s errors? Did we do something wrong in our routing?

issue_title: Bug in Python2.7 with futurize lib.

It seems having:

if six.PY3:

issue_title: Can't use mutable data types for function defaults
issue_body:
Many of the methods in the API use `{}` as a function default, however this can lead to serious issues. See [this blog post](http://pythonconquerstheuniverse.wordpress.com/category/python-gotchas/) on this python gotcha.  You should use `None` and then, for example in `track` call `all_properties.update(properties or {})`.

Without this, subsequent calls that don't provide properties, after one that does, will send the old properties along, which will lead to all sorts of confusion. :confounded:

issue_title: When SoftDeletableModel is used as base class for a model, default manager is not as expected.
issue_body:
Hi.

Let's have a look at this short code:

```
from django.db import models
from model_utils.models import SoftDeletableModel

class MyModel(SoftDeletableModel):
   # other arbitrary fields
    including_deleted = models.Manager()
```

One would expect that the default manager is `objects` from `SoftDeletableModel`, that is, by default the objects that are retrieved do not include the softly deleted models. This is not the case, however, because default_manager is the one that is encountered [first](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/topics/db/managers/#django.db.models.Model._default_manager) and in this case it is the `including_deleted` manager as it is declared directly in the class itself.

When I put default_manager_name into class Meta, ie:

```
class Meta:
    default_manager_name = 'objects'
```

Then this does not pass migrations because `MyModel has no manager named 'objects'`. I have no idea why this happens. I also tried putting the `objects` Manager directly into the `MyModel` above `include_deleted` yet it still couldn't find it. 

I think the expected behaviour is for `default_manager` to be `objects` when `SoftDeletableModel` is used. Even if the community might not agree it should be possible to set the `default_manager` to `objects` through Meta class, but right now it does not work.

Any idea?

After receiving the response, there is an attempt to call `AbstractObject.create_object` with the data parameter set to a keyvalue list, where it expects a dict.

In `api.build_objects_from_response`, the response is this:
```
dict: {u'data': [{u'timestamp': u'2016-05-31T10:00:00', u'data': [{u'count': 2, u'value': u'null'}], u'aggregation': u'host'},

```

`response` is passed to `objectparser.parse_multiple`, which iterates through the items in `response['data']` and passes them individually to `objectparser.parse_single`. So `objectparser.parse_single` receives:

issue_title: Check if addon is already present in a lower repository
issue_body:
This issue is a suggestion.

I have seen many cases in which a user submits an addon to a repository (lets say Krypton) and the addon is already present in "lower" repositories (e.g. helix). In those cases the check-tool should produce a warning to inform the user of this fact since most of the time the user is either not aware of the presence of the addon in a lower repo, doesn't know recent versions still have access to those lower repos, the changes are so minimal that do not justify a full addon review or the changes are not version specific. 
Finding if the addon already exists in another repo is both a memory exercise or a manual checking step which consumes times and preferably should be avoided.

issue_title: ServiceUnavailable: Failed to read from closed connection
issue_body:
What causes Neo4j to close a connection? I have code of the form, 

```python
with GraphDatabase.driver('bolt://localhost:7687') as driver:
        with driver.session() as session:
            # Execute a number of Cypher statements.

       # Perform other tasks for ~ 10 minutes.
      ...

       with driver.session() as session:
            # Execute a number of Cypher statements.
```

however when I try to execute the second set of Cypher statements I get the following error, 

```
neo4j.exceptions.ServiceUnavailable: Failed to read from closed connection Address(host='127.0.0.1', port=7687)
```

The only remedy I know is to redefine the `GraphDatabase.driver` prior to executing the second block, though I'm unsure why this is necessary. As far as I can tell Bolt does't have a specified timeout.

issue_title: Completion candidates from activated virtual env
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm working on per project virtual env and the completion menu is not populated with objects from the virtual env modules. For exemple, if `pandas` is installed inside a virtual env, and then `import pan<Tab>` (with <Tab> being the key to trigger the completion menu), I get `pattern not found` from my text editor client (neovim).

Then I have tried to look at the pyls code and I came up with the following patch:

```diff
diff --git a/pyls/workspace.py b/pyls/workspace.py
index caf4124..f0e54ae 100644
--- a/pyls/workspace.py
+++ b/pyls/workspace.py
@@ -80,7 +80,13 @@ class Workspace(object):
         """
         files = config.find_parents(self._root_path, path, ['setup.py']) or []
         path = [os.path.dirname(setup_py) for setup_py in files]
-        path.extend(sys.path)
+        try:
+            venv = os.environ['VIRTUAL_ENV']
+        except KeyError:
+            path.extend(sys.path)
+        else:
+            venv_sys_path = jedi.evaluate.sys_path.get_venv_path(venv)
+            path.extend(venv_sys_path)
         return path
```

Now, the `pandas` candidate appears in the completion menu.
I'm not sure if my fix is correct or If I missed something obvious when working with virtual env, so I prefer to keep it as an issue instead of a pull request.
It would be nice to keep PyPI releases and git tags in sync :)

issue_title: Put openquake.cfg in a proper place when installed via pip
issue_body:
Currently `oq` looks for `openquake.cfg` in `/etc/openquake` and in `site-packages`. This does not play well with installations from pip because:

1. It is not part of the python package 
2. It cannot be installed in `/etc`
3. `site-packages` folder is the wrong place (out of the openquake context)

To work around this issue in various installers we are setting the `OQ_SITE_CFG_PATH` env var (see https://github.com/gem/oq-installers/blob/master/unix/env/install.sh#L75 and https://github.com/gem/oq-installers/blob/master/windows/nsis-installer/oq-console.bat#L6)

Out[42]: 'ftpmaster@ubuntu.com'
```

However, trying to use the string-formatting does not work as it ought to:

```python
In [43]: "{}".format(k2.userids[0])
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-43-65bf4aba81dd> in <module>()
----> 1 "{}".format(k2.userids[0])

PGPy/pgpy/pgp.py in __format__(self, format_spec)
    631     def __format__(self, format_spec):
    632         if self.is_uid:
--> 633             comment = six.u("") if self.comment == "" else six.u(" ({:s})").format(self.comment)
    634             email = six.u("") if self.email == "" else six.u(" <{:s}>").format(self.email)
    635             return six.u("{:s}{:s}{:s}").format(self.name, comment, email)

TypeError: non-empty format string passed to object.__format__
```

The problem, as it turns out, is a very simple one. I didn't pay enough attention to what I was doing, it would seem:

```python
In [46]: k2.userids[0].comment == ""
Out[46]: False

In [47]: type(k2.userids[0].comment)
Out[47]: NoneType
```

The fix is very simple, but i'm listing four tasks for this issue:

- [ ] Make sure that the regular expression in UserID.parse is consistent in how it assigns missing comment and email (i.e. `None` vs `""`)
- [ ] Either change UserID.parse to assign "" instead of None, or change from `if self.comment == ""` to `if self.comment is None` and probably also `if self.email == ""` to `self.email is None`
- [ ] Coverate didn't cue me that this may not have been completely tested because I used the ternary operator to avoid the ugliness of chained `if`s. However, having those chained `if`s could have prevented this bug from making it past unit testing/ci, so I will change it.
- [ ] Ensure that the unit tests are actually testing all of the possible branches for `PGPUID.__format__`

issue_title: Pass data to before_patch and before_post
issue_body:
Currently, received data (`json_data` from http://flask-restless.readthedocs.io/en/stable/customizing.html#request-preprocessors-and-postprocessors)
 is not passed to before_patch and before_post, thus modifying the incoming data is not a possibility.
`flask-restlless` currently supports this,
```
def post_preprocessor(data=None, **kw):
    """Accepts a single argument, `data`, which is the dictionary of
    fields to set on the new instance of the model.

    """
    pass
```
Ref: http://flask-restless.readthedocs.io/en/stable/customizing.html#request-preprocessors-and-postprocessors

issue_title: readable() is inconsistent across python versions
issue_body:
I was looking to copy what `readable()` in order to add a similar `writable()` function, but it has inconsistent behavior across python versions.  `readable()` will tell you if the file was opened readable (or rather if `read()` will actually succeed), whereas the code path in python2 will simply tell you if the fileobj has a read method, without any comment on whether or not read will fail.  Consider:

```
Python 2.7.11 (default, Jan 22 2016, 08:29:18)
>>> from s3transfer.compat import readable
>>> readable(open('/tmp/asdfafgafdsfd', 'w'))
True

Python 3.5.1 (default, Jan 22 2016, 08:54:32)
>>> from s3transfer.compat import readable
>>> readable(open('/tmp/adfasfasdfd', 'w'))
False
```

I would expect us to try, within reason, to have consistent behavior across python versions.

issue_title: pyvmomi only returns and accepts URL encoded parameters
issue_body:
When using a mixture of PowerCLI and pyvmomi, it's no longer possible to treat code the same because of the following difference in behavior of PowerCLI 10.x:

## Example

PowerCLI (version < 10.x) Get-VDPortrgroup cmdlet returns a / as %2f

Now:
• Portgroup in vCenter UI:  10.1.1.0/24 (App)
• PowerCLI 10.x returns 10.1.1.0/24 (App) 
• PowerCLI 6.3.x returns 10.1.1.0%2f24 (App) 
• pyvmomi 6.7/ansible 2.4 seems to only honor the %2f

Error from pyvmomi/ansible:
```Network '10.1.1.0/24 (App)' does not exists```

## Possible solution/workarounds
- Change default behavior of pyvmomi to match PowerCLI 10.x behavior (return and accept non URL-encoded values such as / instead of %2f)
- Allow override of URL encoding requirement to instead use non-URL encoded

if locator.startswith('//')

issue_title: Race condition when creating multi BalancedConsumer
issue_body:
```python
    def _add_self(self):
        """Register this consumer in zookeeper.
        This method ensures that the number of participants is at most the
        number of partitions.
        """
        participants = self._get_participants()
        # NOTE: Race condition here
        if len(self._topic.partitions) <= len(participants):
            raise KafkaException("Cannot add consumer: more consumers than partitions")

        self._zookeeper.create(
            self._path_self, self._topic.name, ephemeral=True, makepath=True)
```
As shown above, [there is](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L412) a race condition when multi processes create BalancedConsumer at the same time with the same `consumer_group`.

When a race condition occurred, the consumer instance holds [empty partition](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L450), and the internal `_consumer ` is [not inited](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L464).

Moreover, because of the [null](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L250) `_consumer`, there seems to be no way to stop it.

May be we can stop the consumer and raise an exception when [calling _decide_partitions](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L450) returns empty partition?

There should be a way developed for doing pre/post scripting in the virtualenv setup.

issue_title: Updating subspacematrix error handling with VASP
issue_body:
In the `custodian/vasp/handlers.py` script, there is an error-handling method for the "subspacematrix" error (i.e. "WARNING: Sub-Space-Matrix is not hermitian in DAV").  As it stands right now, the custodian file tries to change LREAL when this error occurs. I have found that in systems I have studied, this error often occurs when PREC is not set to Accurate. I have attached an example set of input files. It runs with PREC = Accurate but has the subspace error after 7-10 SCF iterations when PREC is not set (i.e. it's the default value of Normal). It therefore may be desirable to add a PREC switch for this error message. Note that this may be dependent on the particular VASP build. I've tested this on VASP 5.4.1 for the record.
[INCAR.txt](https://github.com/materialsproject/custodian/files/1519019/INCAR.txt)

issue_title: Change 2D Hessian matrix order from y/x to r/c
issue_body:
Hessian matrix computation used to return the matrix elements as "Hxx, Hxy, Hyy", which is equivalent to "Hcc, Hrc, Hrr". This goes against our array order conventions, for no particular reason other than history. See [this comment](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/2194#r71079486) for more context.

Currently, this means that the 2D Hessian matrix is returned in a different order to the 3D+ matrix. This is messy and annoying.

Many functions and tests depend on this output, and the deprecation path is not going to be fun, but I would say that this is a necessary change in the long term (e.g. before 1.0).

issue_title: LineModelND assumes optimal fit runs through data center

issue_title: Cornice CORS doesn't seem to work for resources
Cornice doesn't pass the context through
issue_body:
Here https://github.com/mozilla-services/cornice/blob/master/cornice/service.py#L535-L536

We should always provide the context.

Currently, Pyramid has a way to register exception views (http://docs.pylonsproject.org/projects/pyramid-cookbook/en/latest/pylons/exceptions.html#exception-views). The main drawback to using Pyramid's exception views is that you have to create a new Response object for each view. It would be nice if Cornice could provide a way to register exception views that pass in the existing response object so a new one doesn't have to be created. Something like this would be nice:

```python
@view_config(context=InvalidCreditCardError)
def bad_card(exc, request, response):
    response.errors.add('billing', 'formError', 'You entered an invalid credit card')
    response.errors.status = 501
    return response
```

Notice how the existing response object is passed into the function and errors and status can be easily set without creating a new Response object.

* signature
* attachment
* ...

issue_title: Wrong patch format in accordin to the standard RFC 6902
issue_body:
This is the test that I run
``` python
self.root= {}
self.head= {'foo': 'baz1'}
self.update= {'foo': 'baz2'}

non_list_merger = Merger(self.root, self.head, self.update, {})
try:
    non_list_merger.run()
except UnresolvedConflictsException as e:
    print e.content
```
This is the result of the previous code:
``` Python
[Conflict(('add', '', [('foo', 'baz1')]), ('add', '', [('foo', 'baz2')]))]
```
In according to the standard RFC 6902 that defines: a JSON document structure for expressing sequence of operations to apply to a JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) document; (https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6902)

IMHO the result is wrong for 2 reasons:

1.  It is not wrapped in an object (but is a minor issue, tuple are fine)

2. The format of the response is wrong because is not returning the key `foo` in the right place. This force users to handle different cases and build manually the path for a given patch.

issue_title: fix issue with superuser and parameters
issue_body:
Here is the deal: in Invenio, if there is no explicit restrictions, it means that the action should be available for everybody. Thus, when we have the following table:

| user | action | parameter |
|------|--------|-----------|
| 1    | viewrecord   | 5         |

it means that all the record are freely accessible, except the record 5 that can only be seen by the user 1.

And it works!!!

**The problem**

The problem comes when we have a superuser. consider the following table:

| user | action     | parameter |
|------|------------|-----------|
| 1    | viewrecord | 5         |
| 0    | superuser  |           |

We now have a superuser, which is able to access everything. What should it change from before? now, all the record should be freely viewable, except the record 5 accessible only by the user 1 (explicit action) and the user 0 (superuser).

**BUT** this is not true. Now **ALL** the records are restricted! and only the superuser can see them (except for the record 5 that can also be seen by the user 1).

**The source of the problem**

As you can see here: https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-principal/blob/master/flask_principal.py#L328-L339, Flask-Principal allows a user to access a resource if there is a common value between what the user provides and what the action needs.

As long as there is no superuser, both sets will be empty for the records (except for the record 5).

But as soon as we add a superuser, all the actions will have a set with the superuser inside! See https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-access/blob/master/invenio_access/permissions.py#L85

**Tests for it**

tests already exists and are currently commented because they obviously don't pass (see #56): https://github.com/remileduc/invenio-access/blob/c677ee4d5592829c67835ca8864122dc349cc6ea/tests/test_invenio_access_permissions.py#L181-L190
cc @drjova @greut

issue_title: api: improve API for bucket creation
issue_body:
**Problem:**
It is not intuitive that accessing `FilesMixin.files` will create a bucket if there is no bucket. A getter should not modify the database.

For B2Share deposits we want to create a bucket for every deposit. The bucket can later be deleted if the deposit is published without any file. The only way to do that with FilesMixin API is to call `deposit.files`. This looks more like a dead line of code than the creation of a bucket.

What's more, there is no way to know if a bucket exist with the API. One has to directly query the model. If, like I did, somebody tries to see if there are files in the serializer of invenio-records-rest, a bucket will be created automatically instead of saying that there are no files. This can create important bugs in case of rollback.

**Suggestion:**

- Add a method for creating the bucket. It could be a setter which takes a dict like this:
```
record.files = { 'hello.txt': BytesIO(b'Hello world!') }
```
- The `files` getter should not create a bucket if there is none. It can either return an iterator which ends immediately or return None. If an iterator is returned the bucket of the iterator should be `None`. That way it is explicit that there are no bucket.

issue_title: api: resolve Record API subclassing limits
issue_body:
The class `invenio_files_records.api.Record` adds a feature to `invenio_records.api.Record` which is the  `RecordsBuckets` deletion when `Record.delete(...)` is called.

**Question:**
What do we do for Invenio module generic enough to use directly the `invenio_records.api.Record`? If they call `invenio_records.api.Record.delete(...)` the bucket won't be deleted.
On the other hand if we make every module use `invenio_files_records.api.Record` then they can't work without `invenio_records.api.Record`. Thus Tind which has its own files module won't be able to use many Invenio modules.
We can make the class configurable as it is now in Deposit. But then it should become a pattern which we use in every module.

cc @remileduc @PXke

What do you think @jirikuncar @lnielsen?

As far as I can see, zdaemon never reopens the files configured in the <eventlog> section, so you cannot rotate them externally (e.g. with logrotate) without restarting zdaemon.

I intend to change the `logreopen` and `reopen_transcript` commands to also call `ZConfig.components.logger.loghandler.reopenFiles()`.

issue_body:
i found can not call shell() and logcat() same time, like this case:
`import os.path as op

from mainfuzzer.adb import adb_commands
from mainfuzzer.adb import sign_m2crypto

# KitKat+ devices require authentication
signer = sign_m2crypto.M2CryptoSigner(
    op.expanduser('~/.android/adbkey'))
# Connect to the device 
device = adb_commands.AdbCommands.ConnectDevice( rsa_keys=[signer])

class LogcatTask:
    """
    inner class for logcat thread task
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self._running = True
        self.output = ''

    def terminate(self):
        self._running = False

    def run(self, options, timeout_ms):
        logcat = device.Logcat(' '.join(options), timeout_ms=timeout_ms)
        while self._running and timeout_ms > 0:
            self.output += logcat.next().strip()


from threading import Thread
c = LogcatTask()
t = Thread(target=c.run, args=([' ', ], 10000,))
t.start()

cmd = "ls -al /data/local/tmp"
print '=============>' + device.Shell(cmd)

import time
time.sleep(5)

c.terminate()   # Signal termination
t.join()        # Wait for actual termination (if needed)

issue_title: qgrid is not correctly resizing when it is unhidden inside of an accordion widget
issue_body:
When placing a qgrid inside of an Accordion widget which is not initially shown, qgrid columns are not properly resized when the qgrid accordion tab is selected. This issue most likely has to do with qgrid not resizing when its state is changed from hidden to visible. The columns are correctly resized by resizing the window or alternatively resetting the qgrid df.


Example. 

accordion= Accordion(children=[show_grid(df)])
accordion.selected_index = None

when you click the accordion tab, qgrid columns will be sized incorrectly.

If I resize the window, or reset qgrid.df the columns are correctly set again.

s._grid.df = s._grid.df
Use tabulate or qgrid?
