Apologies for re-opening this issue but you have misunderstood the error message and mistakenly closed the bug (https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/issues/592).

Here are the settings that I am using:

```python
INSTALLED_APPS = (
...
'raven.contrib.django.raven_compat',
...
)
```

As you can see, the proper package is being used.

```python
File "/lib/python2.7/site-packages/haystack/utils/app_loading.py", line 22, in is_app_or_model
    raise ImproperlyConfigured("'%s' isn't recognized as an app (<app_label>) or model (<app_label>.<model_name>)." % label)
django.core.exceptions.ImproperlyConfigured: 'raven.contrib.django' isn't recognized as an app (<app_label>) or model (<app_label>.<model_name>).
```

The error message references ```raven.contrib.django``` because that is the label name being used in raven/contrib/django/apps.py (https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/contrib/django/apps.py) which was introduced as of raven==5.2.0:

```python
class RavenConfig(AppConfig):
    name = 'raven.contrib.django'
    label = 'raven.contrib.django'
    verbose_name = 'Raven'
```

As explained in the original bug, the label name uses periods which conflicts with the following Haystack code:

```python
def is_app_or_model(label):
    label_bits = label.split('.')

    if len(label_bits) == 1:
        return APP
    elif len(label_bits) == 2:
        return MODEL
    else:
        raise ImproperlyConfigured("'%s' isn't recognized as an app (<app_label>) or model (<app_label>.<model_name>)." % label)
```

The fix is to not use periods for the label value:

```python
class RavenConfig(AppConfig):
    name = 'raven.contrib.django'
    label = 'raven_contrib_django'
    verbose_name = 'Raven'
```

% conda create -n galsim14 --channel http://conda.lsst.codes/sims --channel defaults --override-channels lsst-galsim

import { faFacebookF, faTwitter } from '@fortawesome/free-brands-svg-icons';

issue_title: installing vsc-base with easy_install (from PyPI) picks up vsc-install from GitHub (rather than PyPI)?
issue_body:
I'm not 100% sure about this, but I see no other way in which `vsc-install` 0.10.20 was picked up during the EasyBuild bootstrap while it was not released yet on PyPI (it is now, since I published the latest `master` of `vsc-install` to deal with the issue), cfr. https://api.travis-ci.org/jobs/177114943/log.txt?deansi=true

@stdweird Is that intentional? Shouldn't packages published on PyPI be using the `vsc-install` that was used to package it, or am I missing something?

If it is intentional, we need a way to ensure that the latest `vsc-install` is always published on PyPI too?
Or I need to make some changes to how the bootstrap picks a `vsc-install` version to install from PyPI during stage2; it now checks what was installed during stage1, and assumes that version is available on PyPI.

issue_title: Supprimer les commentaires des fichiers XML de paramètres
issue_body:
Au cours de l'issue #752 avec @michelbl nous avons remarqué que les fichiers XML de paramètres d'OpenFisca contenaient des commentaires.

Problème : ces commentaires ne sont pas découvrables par l'API web par exemple.

Plusieurs sortes :
- commentaires d'organisation ([exemple](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/068aa0ec610d8d7783c500b43f93866641728954/openfisca_france/parameters/cotsoc.xml#L209)
  ```
  <CODE code="hsup_exo" description="Exonération des heures supplémentaires" format="bool" origin="openfisca"> # TODO: find a better place
  ```
- références législatives ([exemple](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/068aa0ec610d8d7783c500b43f93866641728954/openfisca_france/parameters/cotsoc.xml#L388-L392))
  ```
      <NODE code="commun" description="Communes aux cadres et non cadres" origin="openfisca">
      <!-- Cotisations patronales au titre de la pénibilité
        - https://www.service-public.fr/professionnels-entreprises/vosdroits/F33777
        - https://www.urssaf.fr/portail/home/employeur/calculer-les-cotisations/les-taux-de-cotisations/les-cotisations-patronales-au-ti/cotisations-dues.html
        - Code du travail - Article L4162-19
     -->
  ```
- commentaires de vérifications ([exemple](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/068aa0ec610d8d7783c500b43f93866641728954/openfisca_france/parameters/fonc.xml#L74))
  ```
  <VALUE deb="2006-11-01" valeur="449" /># TODO: check in july 2014 for "fin"
  ```
- des précisions ([exemples](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/068aa0ec610d8d7783c500b43f93866641728954/openfisca_france/parameters/taxation_capital.xml#L206))
  ```
  <NODE code="droits_soc" description="Droits sociaux" origin="openfisca"> ## parts actions détenus par salariés et mandataires sociaux intro en 2006 ##
  ```

Proposition : selon les cas, convertir ces commentaires en attributs XML ou les supprimer.

issue_title: Strange Python 3 behavior after setting attributes
issue_body:
When I define a simple class such as

```python
class block(object):
    pass
```
and then print the size of an instance of that class (using `pympler.asizeof.asizeof`), I get roughly equivalent behavior on Python 2.7.13 (size=344 B) and Python 3.6.1 (size=168 B).

However, if I print the size of an instance of that class after adding a large number of attributes **to an entirely separate instance of that class**, I see very strange behavior with Python 3. In particular, the size of the new, and supposedly empty, instance of that class is huge (almost 100 KB). Is this a bug or am I using `asizeof` wrong?

Here is a complete example:

```python
import pympler.asizeof

def _fmt(num, suffix='B'):
    """format memory output"""
    for unit in ['','K','M','G','T','P','E','Z']:
        if abs(num) < 1000.0:
            return "%3.1f %s%s" % (num, unit, suffix)
        num /= 1000.0
    return "%.1f %s%s" % (num, 'Yi', suffix)

class block(object):
    pass

def f():
    b = block()
    for i in range(10000):
        setattr(b, "c"+str(i), i)

print(_fmt(pympler.asizeof.asizeof(block())))
f()
print(_fmt(pympler.asizeof.asizeof(block())))
```

For Python 2.7.13, this outputs:
```bash
344.0 B
344.0 B
```
For Python 3.6.1, this outputs:
```bash
168.0 B
87.5 KB
```

In `setup.py`, `install_requires` is filled with the content of [`requirements.txt`]( afdko/requirements.txt).
https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/afdko/blob/develop/setup.py#L205
https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/afdko/blob/develop/setup.py#L145-L147

These requirements dependency versions are pinned with version matching, which means some packages may be downgraded to those versions if more recent versions happen to be installed.

Could these be changed to inclusive ordered comparison `>=` or compatible release `~=` instead of version matching `==`?

issue_title: InvalidClient: HTTPBasicAuth with username and password for Legacy Applications
issue_body:
I stumbled upon this error while trying to use requests-oauthlib with an oauth2 server created using django-oauth-toolkit. I think that there is a misunderstanding of section 2.3.1 of the RFC 6749 (here is the link https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-2.3.1) that is also the cause of #221 and #218 

The RFC says:
> Clients in possession of a client password MAY use the HTTP Basic
   authentication scheme as defined in [RFC2617] to authenticate with
   the authorization server.

The `client password` is the `client secret`. How should we create a Basic Auth Header? the RFC says:
> The *client identifier* is encoded using the
   "application/x-www-form-urlencoded" encoding algorithm per
   Appendix B, and the encoded value is used as the username; the *client
   password* is encoded using the same algorithm and used as the
   password.

client_id is used as username.
client_secret is used as password.

So I think that the header should be create as follows (and that is what we expect in django-oauth-toolkit):
```
auth = requests.auth.HTTPBasicAuth(client_id, client_secret)
```
the problem is that `username` and `password` are used instead, which are user's credentials and not the application's credentials.

Also, the RFC example shows the following header
> Authorization: Basic czZCaGRSa3F0Mzo3RmpmcDBaQnIxS3REUmJuZlZkbUl3

If you decode the base64 value you'll get:
```
>>> base64.b64decode('czZCaGRSa3F0Mzo3RmpmcDBaQnIxS3REUmJuZlZkbUl3')
>>> b's6BhdRkqt3:7Fjfp0ZBr1KtDRbnfVdmIw'
```
which are the client_id and client_secret  used in the example at the end of section 2.3.1

Bigtable supports millisecond granularity but using microsecond granularity in TimestampRange

https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/_modules/google/cloud/bigtable/row_filters.html#TimestampRange

Therefore, it was the result in the following error:
```
Timestamp granularity mismatch. Expected a multiple of 1000 (millisecond granularity), but got 1519628106494933.
```

## Code

```python
end = datetime.datetime.now()
start = end - datetime.timedelta(minutes = 60)
timefilter = TimestampRangeFilter(TimestampRange(start=start, end=end))
row_data = table.read_rows(filter_=timefilter)
row_data.consume_all()
```

```

Am I missing a way to declare dependencies for test code?

The `Key` object stands in as per-process unique identifier for a python object... it used to be equivalent to the pickled representation of the object, but is currently generated by storing an association in a python `dict` between an instance of the object and an integer Key value here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/3cfb0f0758d5517f9128cab3a735141294faf13e/src/python/pants/engine/subsystem/native.py#L361-L372

The reason for the change from using the pickled representation to using an integer `Key` was performance. But since then, we've switched from using `Key`s for everything, to using them only where necessary to uniquely identify an object... ie, where a python object is used as a subject (you can look for calls to [key_for](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/3cfb0f0758d5517f9128cab3a735141294faf13e/src/rust/engine/src/nodes.rs#L118) to identify cases where we convert from `Value` to `Key`).

----

Unfortunately, the current definition of `Key` relies on a proper implementation of `__eq__` and `__hash__`. And one important place where we override `__eq__` and `__hash__` for performance is [in Struct](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/3cfb0f0758d5517f9128cab3a735141294faf13e/src/python/pants/engine/struct.py#L261-L282), where to avoid re-hashing the entire struct, we use the `address` directly.

Using `Struct.address` to avoid re-hashing the entire struct is a valuable optimization, but the problem is that if a BUILD file has changed on disk, the resulting Struct for the Targets will have the same address, but _different content_. This is buggy, because we will compute the same `Key` for the changed Struct.

One approach to fixing this would be to replace the `Struct.address` optimization with something that memoizes the hashcode of the struct (for example), but this would still mean that `__eq__` would be very expensive.

----

To avoid needing accurate `__hash__` and `__eq__` and (_potentially_) improve performance, we should explore switching back to pickling for computing object `Key`s. The code that was used to pickle still exists in [storage.py](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/3cfb0f0758d5517f9128cab3a735141294faf13e/src/python/pants/engine/storage.py), but is not currently used.

issue_title: Update Scala platform to fail on underspecified custom setup
issue_body:
When a user specifies a custom version of scala currently portions of ScalaPlatform fall back to defaults if the user doesn't create targets to override the defaults.

For example if the user specifies custom but does not create a //:scalac target pants will not complain but will compile with scala 2.10 which is the default. 

compiler_classpath and style_classpath should provide the user with a useful error message to indicate that scala tools are underspecified for a custom setup.

```
if mfa_device_details['MFADevices'] == []:
            print('User: '+userName+' is NOT-COMPLIANT!')
            eval["ComplianceType"] = "NON_COMPLIANT"
            eval["Annotation"] = "No MFA Device detected for user"

```
        configurationItem = get_configuration_item(invokingEvent)

        if configurationItem is None:

            if isinstance(compliance, list):

issue_body:
### Observed behavior

On a Raspberry Pi 3, I managed to successfully import channel listings and then contents from Sikana.

But for Khan Academy, I've been unsuccessful. While waiting for the channel listings to appear, I get logged out, and logging back in, I cannot tell if the import listing job is still running. I cannot return to the page, but have to go through the same step again. Is it restarting? Are two jobs running simultaneously?

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/374612/38458040-15db6faa-3a99-11e8-9008-cbacbbdd2d58.png)


### Expected behavior

1. **Administrators need a job manager where they can see what's keeping the device busy**
2. **Jobs need to run consecutively**

### User-facing consequences

Especially smaller devices like Raspberry Pi will fail the heavier jobs. The user, not being able to tell what's going on, will kill processes and start over, thinking that jobs are stuck or dead.

### Errors and logs

n/a - this issue is not about running out of memory or failures. This is about tracking progress and managing jobs.

### Steps to reproduce

Khan Academy especially with it's 31k items is heavy enough to showcase.

### Context

0.9.0, Raspbian Jessie, Raspberry Pi 3.

issue_title: API endpoint to return file sizes and node count for existing channels
issue_body:
To show the size of a saved channel on the 'manage content' UI, we compute the number-of-files and total-size by first requesting the entire array of metadata for files associated with a channel. After that, it is then aggregated it on the client; the individual Files entries are not used at all.

For large channels, this array can include thousands of entries and be MBs in size, and take many seconds to fulfill the request.

It might be more performant to send the total file-size and file-count pre-aggregated.

issue_title: Add a clearFilters method to Reads
issue_body: It is possible to add multiple filters to a `Reads` instance, but not to remove filters. It would be more efficient (than re-reading FASTA each time) to be able to remove any filters so that new ones can be added and the `Reads` instance re-iterated.

issue_body:
When i'm trying to save my image using the following python commands:
  cli = Client(base_url='tcp://127.0.0.1:2375')
  image = cli.get_image(new_image)
  image_tar = open('new_image_api.tar', 'w')
  image_tar.write(image.data)
  image_tar.close()

It raise
requests.exceptions.ReadTimeout: HTTPConnectionPool(host='127.0.0.1', port=2375): Read timed out. (read timeout=60)

My docker deamon raise 
Can't add file /var/lib/docker/tmp/docker-export-601225857/032e1789d3ece898230e065002eb5614139f0a8c426a4729c73f4e4a7af7f3c8/layer.tar to tar: io: read/write on closed pipe 

Is there any way of setting up an higher timeout delay since it's a big image (more than 700MB) ?

Hi,

There seems to be a typo on the following line, which is missing a comma.

https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/22b7b76142bd735c6be4f678dda8cf9d413e9f1c/docker/models/services.py#L279

The effect of this, is that

The API documentation (https://cloud.dwavesys.com/qubist/downloads/09-1007A-B_SAPI_REST_WS_Developer_Guide.pdf) is not clear on this point, but *only* active qubits should have bias values set/sent (for inactive qubits, user must send `NaN` in the `lin` list of biases, of course only for existing qubits). Similarly, in the list of couplings `quad`, *only* active couplings should be specified.

So, the `lin` should be of length N (where N is number of working qubits <= 2048 on D-Wave 2 system). Inactive qubits are encoded as `NaN`, active as 64-bit floating point number.

The `quad` list should be of length M, where M is the number of user-defined couplings between qubits.

Related to #151.

issue_title: Missing option to change the output directory
issue_body:
Would help with e.g. the meson build system that expects things to be generated in the same directory as the source file.

It should probably only change the output directory of the user-requested output. So if you have a multiple master .glyphs file and the user specifies `-o otf`, intermediary steps can litter whatever directory they want, but the final font binaries should be put into the directory the user declared. If the user says `-i -o ufo`, the final UFO instances should be put into said directory.

issue_title: Usages of asyncio objects (Future, Task, etc), must be provided with the loop kwarg
issue_body: txio takes an event loop for configuration txaio.config.loop.  Unfortunately, this is only used in the aio.call_later() function.  It should also be used in all asyncio calls (Future, Task, gather, etc).

issue_title: Feature request: Double-exposure or dual-exposure use
issue_body:
I'd like to use trackpy for particle tracking in a double-exposure imaging system used for monitoring high-velocity flows. For these purposes, the flows are fast, but stable over time.

In such a case, a pair of images (image A and image B) is recorded with (e.g.) a 1 microsecond delay. Particles can be tracked between these images. These image-pairs are then recorded at (e.g.) 100Hz. 

If I track particles independently between each image A and image B (e.g. by running pred.link_df and trackpy.relate_frames iteratively inside a loop) then I get wildly different flows between different image pairs - which is wrong, we know the flows are stable.

If I track particles across the whole dataset, then I believe I am linking A-B-A-B-A-B-A-B, but it is inappropriate to link the B-A frames (because they have a different time-base). 

For any case where you'd actually use this technique, you'd expect all the particles to be long gone by the time the next 'A' frame has occured, so it would be sufficiently to have the solution space of velocity vectors connected between adjacent image-pairs.

Is such a thing (already?) possible with trackpy, and if not, could it be implemented?

issue_title: docs: Clarify router SSL vs. SSL per-app
issue_body:
SSL documentation is here: http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/ssl-endpoints/

I've heard a few users who were confused when trying to use both router-level SSL and per-application SSL certificates. As far as I'm aware, configuring router-level SSL certificates will terminate all requests using the single certificate configured there, even if some applications have their own certificates configured.

We should make this behavior clear, and ideally allow application-level SSL certs to override the router-level cert, which should just be a default.

https://github.com/botlink/terraform-provider-deis

issue_title: a way to pull configs into another application
issue_body:
to run migrations on my app, "services", i create another application "services-migrations". when i change models, i push to "services-migrations", run the db migration and then push to "services".

this works if "services" and "services-migrations" have the same configs (ie. DATABASE_URL).

this process is the same for any of the environments i create (qa/staging/production). i think it would be useful to easily copy the settings from one application to another.

this works, but it could be better supported IMO:

```
deis config:list -a services --oneline | grep -v == | xargs deis config:set -a services-migrations
```

i'm happy to add if i can get pointed in the right direction if you think its useful.

At the moment, we build `deis/registry` from complete scratch, importing the source code via retrieving the souce code from a git sha and constructing the image. This goes against The Docker Way in which we should be doing configuration *on top* of deis/docker-registry. To give an example, the registry Dockerfile should look like the following:

```
FROM deis/docker-registry:import-repository

ADD . /app
WORKDIR /app
CMD /app/bin/boot
```

and let upstream handle the rest.

I've been running deis now for about three months with fairly frequent releases, in a DigitalOcean environment. I've had three major outages due to Deis:

 - The first cluster I put up became catatonic after etcd blew up and couldn't be saved (Deis is still on a version that's vulnerable)
 - The load balancer stopped forwarding to apps
 - The upgrade to 1.5.2 took about 30 minutes to complete -- too long imo. Should be sub-second outage.

I'm currently experiencing a third partial outage due to the drives on the builder machine having been filled up. The docs helped me free 400 MiB, but that's not enough, so I can't make progress (I've been waiting about an hour on `du` to finish, and meanwhile just using du crashed docker and that made deisctl fail completely (SSH timeout)).

I don't particularly like operating this fire service and I need to be sure that my customers can get to their apps.

I have these invariants:

 - I don't care about old releases much, most of the time. If I do, it's normally a rollback.
 - I don't want to have to manually clean disks - the deis platform should not roll itself over the edge of the cliff unless I allow it explicitly, but that's what it's currently doing
 - I don't care about saving old logs on disk -- I want to ship logs and I've set up papertrail - but I would much prefer semantic logging over "text" logging - i.e. Logentries or [Logary](https://logary.github.io/). I could take potentially ownership of this as I've written logary and have a fairly good idea of how to deal with logs.

Therefore I would suggest a 'stateless plx' option:

 - `deis releases prune` - remove all but the latest and also remove all docker containers not in use
 - no systemctl logs saved to disk by default - make ceph an add-on and expose it with *service discovery* for my apps instead
 - supply a default log router ([Heka](https://github.com/mozilla-services/heka) or Logstash) with op-/user-facing, mutable-after-deploy configuration, and configure it BOTH for metrics and for logs, *semantically* so that you don't have to parse the line of log in every log sink
 - supply a default [alerting framework](https://sensuapp.org/docs/latest/events) out of the box so I know when the platform is going belly-up -- and ensure log-levels and tags are used throughout the code-base: { verbose, debug, info, error, fatal } are my normal levels and fatal errors I always send text messages from -- I can write a logary target for 46elks and have texts available to deis users
 - supply a default backup engine such as [bacula](http://blog.bacula.org/) and have the CLI that configures the platform take a S3 API endpoint + credentials + encryption phrase
 - supply an inverse/corresponding 'provision from backup' command that invokes [the machinery](http://www.bacula.org/5.1.x-manuals/en/main/main/Restore_Command.html)
 - schedule the restart of services in upgrades so that downtime is minimised
   * restart routers last
   * coordinated restarts of routers to let round-robin DNS do its thing

This is the fourth outage and I'm currently setting up the environment without Deis now.

issue_title: Feature request: Allow passing parameters to oauth2_get_dependent_tokens()
issue_body: In the near(-ish) future we'd like clients to pass additional parameters to the dependent token call. For example, specifying `access_type=online` to indicate the client doesn't want or need refresh tokens is supported today. Soon, you'll also be able to pass a `scope` parameter (similar to other OAuth2 calls) to indicate which specific scopes you're interested in receiving dependent tokens for.

issue_title: Fixed length string type is valid?
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

It appears that eth-abi considers a string with a fixed length to be a valid type:
https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/eth_abi/utils/parsing.py#L57-L61

However, eth-abi handles fixed length strings no differently than dynamic-length strings:
https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/eth_abi/encoding.py#L66-L67
https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/eth_abi/decoding.py#L52-L53

### How can it be fixed?

Perhaps the type validation code should be updated at some point to throw an error for fixed length strings?

Most likely not the right project for this, but I couldn't think of the best project (between eth-tester, web3.py, and populus) for this feature suggestion when it comes down to implementation.

---

There should be a standard method to measure meaningful code-coverage metrics (Statement, Branch, and MC/DC ideally) and profile gas costs during runtime execution on EVM bytecode. Doing so on EVM bytecode can be more accurate than against source code (see [here](http://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/6698891/)), and a common implementation we can avoid duplication of efforts between the different langauges. With bytecode-level metrics, one can simply collect the results and figure out the bytecode to source code mapping for a specific language for reporting purposes, which might be integrated into a compiler or external tool for presentation to the user.

Gas cost profiling might not make sense in this context, because IIRC they should be more or less standard for a given set of opcodes (basically everything except external calls). However, I think it might actually be useful to profile at this level given that the run-time execution would figure out the costs of external calls. Collecting this cost for each transaction made since a baseline (e.g. start of testing) could report min/max/average costs for these external calls.

The best reasoning I can give for why this would be good to include in eth-tester is that it can provide this functionality at the transaction-level (basically "runtime"), which is more appropiate for testing than via static analysis methods or implementations alongside source code (like solidity-coverage).

If there is indeed tools for doing this already that are easy to use and embeddable in the testing process, please let me know. I need them for my current project.

issue_title: Conda install doesn't pick up the latest version of a package
issue_body:
We're encountering a problem where `conda` doesn't select the most recent available version of a package from a channel. All the dependencies exist on the channel (i.e., when I add the version number to `conda install` line, the install succeeds), and the versions seem to compare correctly (see below). Could this be a solver bug?

Details: I have the following on the channel:
```
[mjuric@lsst-dev ~]$ conda search lsst-sims --full-name
Fetching package metadata: ......
lsst-sims                    master_gac1855abae.0125    ac1855abae_0  http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/
                             master.20150626170402.post157    ac1855abae_0  http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/
                             master.20150928224422.post8    c2d9827e8b_0  http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/
                             master.20150928224422.post46    c2d9827e8b_0  http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/
```

Testing by comparing `conda.resolve.normalized_version()`-s of all of these confirms that `master.20150928224422.post46` is the highest version:
```
In [1]: from conda.resolve import normalized_version as nv
In [2]: a = nv('master_gac1855abae.0125')
In [3]: b = nv('master.20150626170402.post157')
In [4]: c = nv('master.20150928224422.post8')
In [5]: d = nv('master.20150928224422.post46')

In [6]: a < b, b < c, c < d
Out[6]: (True, True, True)

In [7]: a < c, b < d
Out[7]: (True, True)

In [8]: a < d
Out[8]: True

In [9]: a > b, b > c, c > d
Out[9]: (False, False, False)

In [10]: a > c, b > d
Out[10]: (False, False)

In [11]: a > d
Out[11]: False
```

However, when I run the install, this is what I get (abbreviated output; I've pasted the full output [here](https://gist.github.com/mjuric/b59eedfbf2198c07d78f)):
```
[mjuric@lsst-dev ~/public_html/conda]$ conda --debug install lsst-sims
DEBUG:conda.fetch:channel_urls=('http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/', 'http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/noarch/', 'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/', 'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch/', 'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64/', 'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch/')

...[snip]...

Package plan for installation in environment /ssd/mjuric/projects/lsst_packaging_conda/miniconda:
....
    lsst-sims:                     master.20150626170402.post157-ac1855abae_0
....

Proceed ([y]/n)?
```
. When I specify the version explicitly, it works:
```
[mjuric@lsst-dev ~]$ conda --debug install lsst-sims=master.20150928224422.post46
Fetching package metadata: ......
....
    lsst-sims:                     master.20150928224422.post46-c2d9827e8b_0
...
Proceed ([y]/n)? n
```
(so I don't think it's an issue with missing dependencies).

The channel in question is public -- see http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx for the channel.
The full outputs of the above commands is at https://gist.github.com/mjuric/b59eedfbf2198c07d78f

PS: Do let me know if there's anything else I can try or provide you with to make debugging easier; happy to help!
PPS: Discussion of this problem begun [on issue 1919](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1919#issuecomment-177105531); @mcg1969 advised to open a separate issue, as it's likely this is a different problem.

issue_title: Incorrect signature used when calling to "yaml.load" definition.
issue_body:
Hi,

This is happening with the following installer: *Anaconda2-4.2.0-Windows-x86_64.exe*

I spotted an issue when working with `.condarc` file which triggers a call to `yaml.load` definition with incorrect signature: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/common/yaml.py#L26

The traceback is as follows:

```python
C:\Users\thomas>activate python-2.7.13
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\Scripts\conda-script.py", line 5, in <module>
    sys.exit(conda.cli.main())
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\cli\main.py", line 154, in main
    activate.main()
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\cli\activate.py", line 160, in main
    prefix = prefix_from_arg(sys.argv[3], shelldict=shelldict)
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\cli\activate.py", line 48, in prefix_from_arg
    from conda.base.context import context, locate_prefix_by_name
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\base\context.py", line 561, in <module>
    context = Context(SEARCH_PATH, APP_NAME, None)
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\common\configuration.py", line 707, in __init__
    self._set_search_path(search_path)
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\common\configuration.py", line 725, in _set_search_path
    self._set_raw_data(load_file_configs(search_path))
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\common\configuration.py", line 367, in load_file_configs
    raw_data = odict(kv for kv in chain.from_iterable(load_paths))
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\collections.py", line 69, in __init__
    self.__update(*args, **kwds)
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\_abcoll.py", line 571, in update
    for key, value in other:
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\common\configuration.py", line 367, in <genexpr>
    raw_data = odict(kv for kv in chain.from_iterable(load_paths))
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\common\configuration.py", line 343, in _file_yaml_loader
    yield fullpath, YamlRawParameter.make_raw_parameters_from_file(fullpath)
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\common\configuration.py", line 331, in make_raw_parameters_from_file
    ruamel_yaml = yaml_load(fh)
  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\common\yaml.py", line 26, in yaml_load
    return yaml.load(string, Loader=yaml.RoundTripLoader, version="1.2")
TypeError: load() got an unexpected keyword argument 'version'
```
`version` is not an argument that exists in the conda shipped version of *yaml*, changing the offending line to:

> return yaml.load(string, Loader=yaml.RoundTripLoader)

fixes the issue.

Cheers,

Thomas

issue_title: Unable to create new anaconda environment with conda
issue_body:
I installed Anaconda2-2.5.0 for windows 64 bit. The install went fine and I have a functioning python 2.7 environment. When I try to create a python3 anaconda environment using conda I get the following errors:

`conda create -n py34 python=3.4 anaconda`

This fails during linking with:

```
ipython-qtcons 100% |###############################| Time: 0:00:00 1.31 MB/s
ipywidgets-4.1 100% |###############################| Time: 0:00:00 2.68 MB/s
spyder-2.3.8-p 100% |###############################| Time: 0:00:00 8.39 MB/s
jupyter-1.0.0- 100% |###############################| Time: 0:00:00 562.04 kB/s
spyder-app-2.3 100% |###############################| Time: 0:00:00 2.53 MB/s
anaconda-2.5.0 100% |###############################| Time: 0:00:00 1.41 MB/s

Extracting packages ...
[ COMPLETE ]|##################################################| 100%
Linking packages ...
The system cannot find the path specified.#### | 46%
Error: Error: post-link failed for: qt-4.8.7-vc10_5
```

I have then tried to force install of qt and pyqt with no success:

```
conda install -f qt
Using Anaconda Cloud api site https://api.anaconda.org
Fetching package metadata: ....
Solving package specifications: ....

The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
qt
Use "conda info " to see the dependencies for each package.
```

Also if I do a conda list on the new environment any package that comes alphabetically after pyqt is not installed. This fails with other versions of python as well (I've tried 3.5 and 2.7). It also fails if I create a new empty environment and try to install anaconda in the new environment.

This is gonna be somewhat personal and ranty, consider yourself warned.

I'm going through the fast.ai course, and they use conda for managing dependencies. Yet another dependency manager, hooray. As is pip wasn't good enough. Perhaps it's gonna be really good though?

"Package, dependency and environment management for any language" - well that sounds awesome. How do I get started? Well, first I need to choose whether I want Anaconda or Miniconda. How would I know?

So I look at both Anaconda and Miniconda to compare which one is going to be more pleasant to install under my particular Debian-based Linux distro. Unfortunately, the answer here is: *neither*. "**Download a mysterious shell script and run it**" is the official way to install both Anaconda and Miniconda. I forgive those who don't know any better. But you good people are supposed to be "package, dependency, and environment management" experts. And you want me to install your nice package by running a shell script? Are you out of your mind?

issue_title: conda only updates itself in the root environment
issue_body:
I was listening to a presentation about conda a couple of days ago where it was mentioned that conda aggressively auto-updates itself. Aggressive auto-updates can be problematic, because they can result in the quick spread of a buggy release that is no longer able to update itself, which has happened to me and co-workers in the past.  But in general they are a good thing, because they can help make sure that the installed user base is (a) not using outdated versions where problems were fixed long ago, and (b) are using a similar set of versions so that we don't have to reason about the effects of all possible version combinations.  

However, the main benefits of aggressively auto-updating only accrue if all or nearly all installations auto-update, which does not appear to be the case.  Right now, it looks to me like auto-updates happen only when people regularly use the root environment.  In my own workflows, I haven't installed anything in the root environment in many months; I always work on one of a number of more specialized environments instead.  I didn't even realize this until recently, when I ran into a bug fixed several months ago in conda, and which I expected not to apply to me.  I eventually fixed it by switching to the root environment and running ``conda update conda``, which was fine, but it was not at all clear that this would be needed.  I imagine other users who don't use the root environment may encounter similar issues.  I don't think conda is installed in any environment other than root, but maybe the other environments could warn on a ``conda install`` command if conda itself is more than a month or so out of date?

Dear conda team,

I installed conda with python 3.6, my conda version is 4.3.21 and added a `py27` environment using by executing `$ conda create -n py27 python=2.7`. I am using Windows 10, 64 bit.

issue_title: [Enhancement] Add option to show compiler and runtime configuration
issue_body:
This is highly GnuCOBOL specific but it would be a nice addition.
If there's something wrong with the compiler/runtime it's important to know more details about the actual compiler/runtime. The standard display of ``cobc/cobcrun --version`` is often information enough but something like a button "more details" (where it's possible to view/save the information) would be nice.

Call ``cobc/cobcrun --info`` and ``cobcrun --runtime-env`` for the actual data (the options for cobcrun are relatively new and not implemented in earlier versions).

issue_title: Test Scanner is filename case sensitive
issue_body:
I thought that based on previous discussion, including the `runner_cfg` generic would be enough for VUnit to pickup a testbench to run tests on. It appears that both the entity and filename of the testbench must also meet the following rules:

```
def tb_filter(entity):
    """
    Filters entities with both file name and entity name matching tb_* or *_tb.
    """
    # Above docstring can show up in ui.py warnings
    file_ok = basename(entity.file_name).startswith("tb_") or splitext(basename(entity.file_name))[0].endswith("_tb")
    entity_ok = entity.name.startswith("tb_") or entity.name.endswith("_tb")

    if file_ok and entity_ok:
        return True
    return False
```

This was a problem for me because a testbench was named `myblock_TB.vhd` instead of `myblock_tb.vhd`.

I suggest that the scanner allow any case when filtering on tb_* or *_tb.

It might be worth considering bypassing the naming rules if the entity contains the `runner_cfg` generic.

Regardless, a note should also be added to the documentation that the testbench files and entities _must_ be named in a specific way in order for the scanner to pick them up.

We have found that it is beneficial, in some cases, to set older indexes to Read Only to reduce overhead in our clusters.  Our use case is billions of documents per hour in hourly partitioned indexes.   Index Commands used:

indexname/_settings" -d ' { "index" : { "blocks.write" : "true", "codec.bloom.load" : "false" } }'

issue_title: Action allocation does not apply correctly to all indices

issue_body:
Elasticsearch: 5.2.1
Curator: 4.2.6

Been using curator to prune our Log cluster, however we recently started storing Monitoring data from xPack there. I wanted to start pruning those indices as well.

We use a slightly modified version of [this code](https://gist.github.com/jarpy/f65b24209e0a9240207599a73ce99bc9#file-serverless-curator-py) from [this blog post](https://www.elastic.co/blog/serverless-elasticsearch-curator-on-aws-lambda).

Currently all the logs are getting cleaned up, however when adding the prefix `.monitoring-es-2-` to the cleanup list, nothing occurs.

I checked the indices being pulled in by the initial IndexList creation, and its all of our logs indices, but none of the indices that start with `.`.

Code snippet in question:
```
index_list = curator.IndexList(es)
print (index_list.working_list())
```

That just returns all indices except those with starting with `.`. Is there some way to override that, that I may have missed in the docs somewhere? Or is this something by design? Or a bug? or PEBKAC?

issue_title: [Install] Dev_setup.py doesn't install the latest version of KeyVault
issue_body: KeyVault has an updated management client, version 0.30.0 that includes support for certificates. Our current requirements files import azure2.0.0rc6, which includes azure-mgmt-keyvault0.30.0rc6 (the older version). When I include the new version in the requirements files, which should force pip to install the updated version as part of the dev setup, I instead end up with the rc6 version. issue_title: Idea: Single resource 'show' commands should auto-expand
issue_body:
If we provide list vs single-item output (`list` vs. `show`), the single-item output should automatically --expand.  

I don't know of any cases that support nested expands (generally would require crossing RP boundaries), but we can cap this at the first level of nesting.   

This would align vm instance-view with vm show as a single command.  For perf-sensitive scenarios, we would direct users to `list`.  This would also grow the output size, but this is already an issue across the board and should be addressed by `--terse`

According to https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/azure/hh264517.aspx
REST API returns a Response Header with the request-id.
Then it is possible to track the operation status like any other async op (as mentioned in the same doc).

In delete_storage_account() patching the request from:
```
return self._perform_delete(
            self._get_storage_service_path(service_name))
```
to
```
return self._perform_delete(
            self._get_storage_service_path(service_name), async=True)
```
should to the trick.

PR on the way.

issue_title: list_blobs does not work if prefix is empty string
issue_body: ### Which service(blob, file, queue) does this issue concern? blob ### What problem was encountered? When calling `BlockBlobService.list_blobs(prefix="")` with `prefix=""` will cause an authentication error to be thrown thrown. ``` azure.common.AzureHttpError: Server failed to authenticate the request. Make sure the value of Authorization header is formed correctly including the signature.ErrorCode: AuthenticationFailed AuthenticationFailedServer failed to authenticate the request. Make sure the value of Authorization header is formed correctly including the signature. RequestId:d7eda6cf-b01e-0024-55e6-e0d309000000 Time:2018-05-01T00:52:44.4779222ZThe MAC signature found in the HTTP request '/LACPvjjxGLO/jhbYW19z/4xlPYlZt9DYMp9Rvb/H88=' is not the same as any computed signature. Server used following string to sign: 'GET x-ms-client-request-id:f4aba85a-4cd9-11e8-b3d1-645106571c89 x-ms-date:Tue, 01 May 2018 00:52:43 GMT x-ms-version:2017-07-29 /rozhdevv2/test comp:list prefix: restype:container'. ``` ### Have you found a mitigation/solution? Yes, the reason is that empty prefix is omitted as part of signing process but since it is included in the query param strings sent to the server, the server is actually verifying the signing with prefix set as empty string, which causes the signatures to mismatch. Note: for table service, please post the issue here instead: https://github.com/Azure/azure-cosmosdb-python.

issue_title: Add hook option to run hook once per changed file
issue_body:
I was trying to add a simple hook file for [cfn-lint](https://github.com/awslabs/cfn-python-lint).

The `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` config file I am testing looks like:

```yaml
-   id: cfn-python-lint
    name: AWSLabs CloudFormation Linter
    entry: cfn-lint
    language: python
    files: \.(json|yaml|yml)$
    language_version: python2
```

Unfortunately, `cfn-lint` only supports testing one file at a time. So currently I'd also need to add a wrapper script that loops over all input files.

It would be nice to instead add a new YAML directive like:

```yaml
    files_per_run: 1
```

Instead, and have `pre-commit` handle this case.

Hello,

I'd like to introduce you to 2 simple pre-commit hook I wrote:
https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks

It felt really simple to write, cleanly architectured and easy to share with colleagues.
So thanks a lot for your work on this !

Now, I have some minor complains:

- **no support for local hook development** : I had to create an initial github repo and then hack my changes in `$HOME/.pre-commit/repoXXXXXX/py_env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pre_commit_hooks/forbid_crlf.py` while working on it. There should be an easier way to do this !

- the **required sha** property in `.pre-commit-config.yaml` : combined to the pain point #-1 above, it was really annoying to have to update it each time I was committing a new version of my hook on github. Couldn't you defined **a default empty value meaning to track the lastest version** ? I know I could use tags to specify stable versions, but it doesn't fit a work-in-progress development situation.

- the lack of instructions on how to develop a hook: your docs **never mention the necessity to have a `setup.py` file with entry points defined**, while you actually need it as a `virtualenv` is used under the hood. My [commit history](https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks/commits/master) illustrates how puzzling developping a hook can become :D

What's your take on all of those issues, and which features would you be ok to integrate given that someone would suggest a pull request ?

And again, thank you very much for this awesome hooks manager !

grep on windows doesn't support `-P`.  Perhaps it's time to rethink the pcre hooks and approach it as python regexes instead.

Related: #133

issue_title: ClientDisconnectedError when server returns buggy redirect
issue_body:
## Long story short

<!-- Please describe your problem and why the fix is important. -->
There are some servers which do redirects in a strange way. Some examples are `http://fwmedia.com/`, `http://ebooks.artistsnetwork.com/`, `http://artistsnetwork.tv/`.
The common point between them is that they return redirect and close connection *immediately* after sending `Location` header:
```
 $ curl -v http://artistsnetwork.tv/
*   Trying 216.59.59.63...
* TCP_NODELAY set
* Connected to artistsnetwork.tv (216.59.59.63) port 80 (#0)
> GET / HTTP/1.1
> Host: artistsnetwork.tv
> User-Agent: curl/7.52.1
> Accept: */*
>
< HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
< Location: http://www.artistsnetwork.tv/
* Curl_http_done: called premature == 0
* Connection #0 to host artistsnetwork.tv left intact
```
Notice there is no empty line returned after `Location` header.

While `requests` library, `curl` and browsers tolerate this behaviour and recognize it as a redirect, `aiohttp.client` fails.

## Expected behaviour

<!-- What is the behaviour you expect? -->
There should be a way to handle such responses. Ideally I think such "unexpectedly broken" connections should be recognized in a special way, so that if there is enough information then we consider them "good".

## Actual behaviour

<!-- What's actually happening? -->
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 220, in _request
    yield from resp.start(conn, read_until_eof)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/client_reqrep.py", line 614, in start
    message = yield from httpstream.read()
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/streams.py", line 625, in read
    result = yield from super().read()
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/streams.py", line 456, in read
    yield from self._waiter
aiohttp.errors.ServerDisconnectedError

The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/asyncio/base_events.py", line 466, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 227, in _request
    raise aiohttp.ClientResponseError() from exc
aiohttp.errors.ClientResponseError
```

## Steps to reproduce

<!-- Please describe steps to reproduce the issue.
     If you have a script that does that please include it here within
     markdown code markup -->
```
import asyncio, aiohttp
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(aiohttp.client.get('http://artistsnetwork.tv/'))
```

## Your environment

<!-- Describe the environment you have that lead to your issue.
     This includes project version, OS, proxy server and other bits that
     are related to your case. -->
```
Python 3.6.0
aiohttp==1.3.3
yarl==0.9.8
```

```

Ability to catch the exception

issue_title: Session objects / connection pulls are worthless
issue_body:
## Long story short

<!-- Please describe your problem and why the fix is important. -->

Most realistic webservers don't act the way you may imagine, so, in practice, every request in this library made through session object ends up invalidating this session.  So, not only session object introduces a lot of complication (one would need to use some sort of mutual exclusion to ensure single session object is created to deal with multiple requests), but it also doesn't do what it's supposed to: on every server I've tried so far, after every requests, successful or not, I get `aiohttp.client_exceptions.ServerDisconnectedError`.

## Expected behaviour

<!-- What is the behaviour you expect? -->

Just deal with life the way it is.  The problems your library has with maintaining connection pull need to be swept under your carpet. They are absolutely of no interest to your users.

## Actual behaviour

<!-- What's actually happening? -->

Tons of meaningless exceptions which require a lot of intricate handling with zero benefit to the user.

## Steps to reproduce

<!-- Please describe steps to reproduce the issue.
     If you have a script that does that please include it here within
     markdown code markup -->

Write any code that reuses session object, run it through some arbitrary selection of sites and see how often you get server disconnect errors.

## Your environment

<!-- Describe the environment you have that lead to your issue.
     This includes aiohttp version, OS, proxy server and other bits that
     are related to your case. 
     
     IMPORTANT: aiohttp is both server framework and client library.
     For getting rid of confusing please put 'server', 'client' or 'both'
     word here.
     -->

Unimportant.

issue_title: New Feature: Pre-processing of downloaded submission files
issue_body:
I would like to propose adding a new feature of being able to pre-process submission files.

For example, institutions using BlackBoard can download assignment submission files in a `.zip` archive with the following filename format (give or take some extra garbage in the filename):
`{couse_id}{assignment_id}{timestamp}.zip`

Each of the files in this archive will have the following filename format (give or take some extra garbage in the filename):
`{assignment_id}{student_id}{timestamp}{upload_filename}.{ext}`

I propose allowing for the following (optional) directories in the nbgrader directory structure:
`{course_directory}/downloaded/archive/{zipfiles}`
`{course_directory}/downloaded/{assignment_id}/extracted/{raw_files}.ipynb`
`{course_directory}/downloaded/{assignment_id}/invalid/{raw_files}.{ext}`

The pre-processing step will do two things:
1. extract the archive file/s to `{course_directory}/downloaded/{assignment_id}/extracted/`
2. process these extracted files into `{course_directory}/downloaded/{assignment_id}/invalid/` or the submitted path `{course_directory}/submitted/{student_id}/{assignment_id}/`

This needs to be done in a pluggable way (similar to what is mentioned in #233) as filenames will vary from user to user. There may also the possible option of automatically being able to update the students in the database, based on this processing.

issue_title: Max, how to optimization objective function trace?
issue_body:
I'm using GPy, and I want to plot a figure like this, how to do this under paramz?
[https://ibb.co/k5fUWT](url)

# Issue
I observed that some information could be lost during the parsing of a BibTeX file due to early exiting of the ```_parse_record``` method.

# Study Case
Let say that we are parsing a BibTeX file containing the following entry:
> @book{booksR:Goulet:introR,
> title = {{Introduction à la programmation en R}},
> author = {{Vincent Goulet}},
> publisher = {{Bibliothèque et Archives Canada}},
> year = {2016},
> ISBN = {978-2-9811416-6-8},
> url = {\url{https://cran.r-project.org/doc/contrib/Goulet_introduction_programmation_R.pdf}}}

Even if it is not a common practice of writing an entry this way, the bibtexparser package should be able to handle it since it still is a valid BibTeX entry.

However, when I parse this entry using your package, I get the following parsed entry:
> Reference Instance
>  ID: 
> 	 booksR:Goulet:introR
>  ENTRY TYPE: 
> 	 book
>  Location: 
> 	 R/booksR.bib
>  Number of tags defined: 
> 	 7
>  Defined tags: 
> 	 author, entrytype, id, isbn, publisher, title, year
>  
> @book{booksR:Goulet:introR,
>  author = {Vincent Goulet},
>  isbn = {978-2-9811416-6-8},
>  publisher = {Bibliothèque et Archives Canada},
>  title = {Introduction à la programmation en R},
>  year = {2016}
> }
We clearly see that the URL field has been ignored.

# Analysis
At first, I thought I was caused by the special treatment done over the URL fields for homogenization purpose. I quickly realized that it was only a simple renaming. I then tried to use the ISBN field as the last field of the entry and the same behavior was observed (the ISBN field was ignored, but the URL field was now available). 

So I started digging deeper into the source code until I discovered how you were extracting each field from a "record/entry". The problem arises from the interaction of different details of the implementation.
- absence of ```}\n``` at the end of the record
- no replacement of ```}}``` by ```},\n}```
- val.count('{') != val.count('}') for the final kv

In short, because the last field of the entry is on the same line than the entry closing bracket and that the file do not have any empty row (```\n```) before the EOF, this information won't be parsed.

# Proposed Solution
Add an additional line break (```\n```) to each record parsed in the prepare record section of the ```_parse_record()``` method.
```{python}
# prepare record
record = '\n'.join([i.strip() for i in record.split('\n')])
# correction starts here
record += '\n'
# correction ends here
    if '}\n' in record:
        logger.debug('}\\n detected in the record. Clean up.')
        record = record.replace('\r\n', '\n').replace('\r', '\n').rstrip('\n')
        # treat the case for which the last line of the record
        # does not have a coma
        if record.endswith('}\n}') or record.endswith('}}'):
            logger.debug('Missing coma in the last line of the record. Fix it.')
            record = re.sub('}(\n|)}$', '},\n}', record)
```
For the moment, anybody facing the same problem or using the bibtexparser package to parse unknown BibTeX format should append a new line to the content of the files parsed before passing them to the bparser.

## Additional comment
The validation of standard BibTeX type should be done using a variable containing the list. This would make the package easier to maintain.

issue_title: Incorrectly identify queryset as not iterable when model manager is a subclass
issue_body:
Since upgrading to version 0.8.0 (and pylint 1.8) we're incorrectly getting an error that a queryset is not iterable:

```
E: 49,29: Non-iterable value MyModel.objects.filter(foo=bar) is used in an iterating context (not-an-iterable)
```

`MyModel` in this case is a subclass of a model that has `objects` set to a subclass of of the usual manager (in particular, it uses `model_utils.managers.InheritanceManager`)

Similar uses where the standard manager is used does not cause this error, and we did not get this error with previous versions of pylint and pylint-django.

issue_title: Frozen Basket after returning back from paypal checkout
issue_body:
When a user goes back(no cancelling) from a third party payment the basket stays as frozen.
I have just solved recovering (thaw) frozen basket if user has one.

Is there any collateral issues with this behavior?  i.e. user enters from another browser when paying so baskets is not frozen anymore?

It is a pain for the user to lose the basket if regrets to make the payment.

Motivation:
- https://groups.google.com/forum/?fromgroups=#!topic/django-oscar/RILldxUBj2g
- https://groups.google.com/forum/?fromgroups=#!searchin/django-oscar/status/django-oscar/jbEzUGJ57g4/owY4SouAr-AJ
- https://groups.google.com/forum/?fromgroups=#!searchin/django-oscar/status/django-oscar/ZYXjif4VbvM/mM6DeMpqw9IJ

Detailed docs are required on how to implement a order processing pipeline.

Here's some user stories to consider
### Example 1

The site takes payment at checkout and so an order is created with a single payment event.  At this stage, all lines have status 'pending'

An admin can then use the dashboard to:
- Mark a subset of 'pending' lines as 'shipped'
- Mark a subset of 'shipped' lines as 'returned' (This could trigger an automatic refund)
### Example 2

The site takes only pre-authorises at checkout and so an order is created with a single payment event.  At this stage, all lines have status 'pending'

An admin can then use the dashboard to:
- Mark a subset of 'pending' lines as 'shipped'.  Before the shipping event is recorded, payment is attempted for the total of the selected lines plus any shipping costs (for the whole order) if this is the first batch to ship.
- Mark a subset of 'shipped' lines as 'returned' (This could trigger an automatic refund)
### Tasks
- [x] Correct the docs on the difference between line and order statuses
- [x] Add ability to manually set an order status in the order dashboard

```

So I learned that with WCF and dotnet core, you have to use a Connected Service. So I imported the WSDL for Address Validation and it went fine. I was able to make that request. Then I imported the WSDL for requesting shipping rates. It compiled fine, but when I go to use it, I get the following exception message:

```Count = error CS0103: The name 'InnerExceptionCount' does not exist in the current context	System.AggregateException```

I googled around and saw issues relating to this same error before, but nothing in the given context, and I'm not quite familiar enough yet with what dotnet core and Connected Service is doing under the hood. I dug through the generated reference source, but I didn't see anything alarming.

I'd be happy to provide more information, I'm just not sure what to show?

issue_title: suggestion - add a text file to archive that includes the domain names
issue_body:
let's assume i create a multi-domain certificate with the letsencrypt client

	./letsencrypt-auto certonly --standalone \
		-d example.com \
		-d foo.example.com \
		-d example2.com

That will store into /etc/letsencrypt the following core files (and some others):

* archive
*  /etc/letsencrypt/archive/{DOMAIN}/cert1.pem
*  /etc/letsencrypt/archive/{DOMAIN}/chain1.pem
*  /etc/letsencrypt/archive/{DOMAIN}/fullchain1.pem
*  /etc/letsencrypt/archive/{DOMAIN}/privkey1.pem
* live
*  /etc/letsencrypt/live/{DOMAIN}/cert.pem
*  /etc/letsencrypt/live/{DOMAIN}/chain.pem
*  /etc/letsencrypt/live/{DOMAIN}/fullchain.pem
*  /etc/letsencrypt/live/{DOMAIN}/privkey.pem

Unless you use `openssl x509` to decode the cert, there's no easy way to tell what is on a cert because only one DOMAIN appears in the directory path.  This makes finding the cert for a specific domain rather difficult, because every active cert must be analyzed to find the right certificate.

So I have a small suggestion:

Add an `info.txt` / `info1.txt` file to the directories which simply contains the following:

    signed: <timestamp>
    expires: <timestamp>
    domains: <list of domains>

This would allow someone to quickly `grep` the `live/*` folders for data on which certificate is where.  

Another crazier solution would be to create a `domains` archive that has directory for each domain, and the contents are just symlinks to files in the /archive/.  using an info.txt would be simpler though...

    grep foo.example.com live/*/info.txt

issue_title: automatic upgrading in letsencrypt-auto downgrades to incompatible version
issue_body:
Tested in latest master, https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/commit/1a77c7385768e73851b2e4e685073fa39d4a2298

**Steps:**

1. git clone https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.git
2. cd letsencrypt
3. ./letsencrypt-auto

`letsencrypt: error: unrecognized arguments: --le-auto-phase2`

Cause: **letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto**
```bash
REMOTE_VERSION=`"$LE_PYTHON" "$TEMP_DIR/fetch.py" --latest-version`
if [ "$LE_AUTO_VERSION" != "$REMOTE_VERSION" ]; then
    echo "Upgrading letsencrypt-auto $LE_AUTO_VERSION to $REMOTE_VERSION..."
    ...
fi
"$0" --le-auto-phase2 "$@"
```
will fetch the version from https://pypi.python.org/pypi/letsencrypt/json. Right now that version is "0.4.0". Since the check above only checks if the versions are different, the auto script will "upgrade" version "0.5.0.dev0" to "0.4.0", which does not know about the new argument, and attempt to run with that.

The changes were introduced in https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/commit/e8f6d4ff39e0d7cd4331a99a8941fc3771c5f07c

In my code I am spawning tons of oct2py instances, as I want clean variables in each, and exiting them when I am done. I know that I could just clear the variables that I need before each subsequent call, but I figured since I tracked this down I would go ahead an write it up. I may try to fix this on my local, but I have had problems with pip install -E in the past unfortunately. 

The problem is that when I call exit on my Oct2Py object, the pty associated with it is not closed. I believe this is because the terminate method of the underlying ptyprocess used by the REPLWrapper is the only method being called, and it doesn't seem to close the file descriptors for some reason. There is a close method on the pexpect ptyprocess that underlies it, and I think all we need to do is also call the close method when terminating the replwrapper. Please correct me if I am misunderstanding something. I am including the files and locations I traced this through at the end of this issue. Thanks for all the work you are doing with this stuff and sorry to open so many issues! 

# Potential ways to recreate:

- enter python repl
- import metakernel
- spawn some processes (in my case using the same args the octave_kernel uses, from 
https://github.com/Calysto/octave_kernel/blob/7399120bfbeaeccf02b30b3a6b68aa7928b4170d/octave_kernel/kernel.py#L351
- terminate them and note that that the number of ptys for the system remains unchanged
- now call close on the child object of the REPLWrapper, and note that the pty and its fd are closed 


# Files and calls
- https://github.com/blink1073/oct2py/blob/c188b719dbefe4a23b214f19dacbca712fc07fa8/oct2py/core.py#L507
- https://github.com/Calysto/octave_kernel/blob/7399120bfbeaeccf02b30b3a6b68aa7928b4170d/octave_kernel/kernel.py#L144
- https://github.com/Calysto/octave_kernel/blob/7399120bfbeaeccf02b30b3a6b68aa7928b4170d/octave_kernel/kernel.py#L351
- https://github.com/Calysto/metakernel/blob/e7a8d817e6443c80ac3f1faaed2cc096cb6572c8/metakernel/replwrap.py#L63
- https://github.com/Calysto/metakernel/blob/e7a8d817e6443c80ac3f1faaed2cc096cb6572c8/metakernel/replwrap.py#L194
- https://github.com/blink1073/oct2py/blob/c188b719dbefe4a23b214f19dacbca712fc07fa8/oct2py/core.py#L100
- https://github.com/pexpect/pexpect/blob/b36c03315fcf9bec711f79098b2fa31dfd264c76/pexpect/pty_spawn.py#L590
- the pty_spawn does not attempt to call close on its underlying ptyprocess, might raise issue there, but they would
- 	probably respond by asking us to just close both their close and terminate methods
- might be easier if we just call close in addition to terminate to actively close the fds associated with the ptyprocess 
- https://github.com/pexpect/pexpect/blob/b36c03315fcf9bec711f79098b2fa31dfd264c76/pexpect/pty_spawn.py#L311

issue_title: tzstr not properly detecting invalid strings
issue_labels: [{'id': 149492525, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dateutil/dateutil/labels/bug', 'name': 'bug', 'color': 'fc2929', 'default': True}, {'id': 149492528, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dateutil/dateutil/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}, {'id': 667039615, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dateutil/dateutil/labels/medium-difficulty', 'name': 'medium-difficulty', 'color': 'fbca04', 'default': False}, {'id': 177050120, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dateutil/dateutil/labels/time%20zones', 'name': 'time zones', 'color': 'c7def8', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
I noticed that [this test](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/test/test_tz.py#L903) is not actually parsing the time zone string correctly because that string format is not supported:

``` python
>>> from dateutil import tz
>>> tz.tzstr("EST5EDT") == tz.tzstr("EST5EDT,4,1,0,7200,10,-1,0,7200,3600")
True
>>> tz.tzstr("EST5EDT") == tz.tzstr("EST5EDT,5,4,0,7200,11,-3,0,7200,3600")
True
```

I'm thinking maybe once this is fixed, these tests should be switched to compare tzstr to tzrange, or to some performance-based tests (like checking that the transitions happen at the right time, etc.

Restructure codebase to move towards a true [Django reusable app](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/intro/reusable-apps/). I'll be handling this over the next couple weeks, but wanted to make an issue to keep everybody in the loop. The goal of this restructure is to make Dispatch easier to distribute outside of The Ubyssey. It will mostly be moving files around and won't require much code change beyond renaming import statements.

- Change `/apps` to `/modules`
- Move `/apps/api` to `/api/v1`
- Rename `/modules/core` to `/module/auth`
- Create a new `/dispatch/models.py` file that imports models from all modules
- Get rid of `/conf/default_settings.py`, find a better solution for defining default settings

After this is complete, users will be able to create an empty Django project and simply include Dispatch in their `INSTALLED_APPS` setting:

```py
# some-project/settings.py

INSTALLED_APPS = (
  'dispatch',
  # ...
)

# Single dictionary to hold all Dispatch settings
DISPATCH = {
  'enable_api': True,
  'admin_url': '/admin'
  # ...
}

issue_title: b2 sync changes file names with accents and latin characters on macos
issue_body:
I am using version 1.1.0 on a MacOS 10.13.3

I have a bucket which I synchronized a while ago, and today I was about to sync again, and noticed that the list of changes that were going to be performed was larger than I expected.

The problem is with files or paths with accented characters or with n "ñ" on them.

Situation:

Remote file (in bucket)
 - /path/accénted/file.foo

Local file
 - /path/accénted/file.foo

Changes to be performed by b2
 - delete /path/accénted/file.foo
 - upload /path/accented/file.foo            s

So b2 is removing the accent from the file/path and tries to find a remote file that matched the modified name, which fails, it then finds the accented file remotly and decides that locally it has no counterpart, so deletes the remote on, and uploads a new one.

Now, the "upload" action has an "s" at the end, which I do not know what it means.

Now, I tested on a new accented file, one that did not exist on the bucket, and it uploads fine, keeps the accent, and subsequently correctly detects that the file exists already, so this seems to be happening with only the old files I have.

We renamed some folders and the folders are having to do a full upload with the new names. I was under the impression before signing up that renaming folders was fine as a fingerprint at both ends was used to know its the same file, and just update the server.

According to the following page, if a file is renamed or moved, it should have a matching fingerprint and just be updated on the server rather than being uploaded again.

Could it be that our sync script is not using the right parameters on the CLI tool or is there a bug in the CLI?

Our content team need to rename a lot of folders, in total these folders contain about 8tb of data, which will take a good 1.5-2 weeks non stop to upload again when renamed, plus the manual job of deleting the old folders from the backblaze b2 server. 

Thank you.

Clint.

https://help.backblaze.com/hc/en-us/articles/217666638-What-Happens-If-I-Move-or-Rename-a-File-

"Our software contains a process called "deduplication" where files are digitally fingerprinted (checksummed) before they are sent to the server. When a file's fingerprint (SHA-1 checksum) matches an already backed up file, but it's renamed or moved (including drive to drive), it's simply updated at the servers, rather than re-transmitted.

You may see these files queued for back up, but once any given file is at the front of the queue, it will be checksummed, compared against the existing backup, and "deduplicate" resulting in it being updated on the server rather than re-uploading"

issue_title: Support Schemas for Request Body / Query Params
issue_body:
The marshmallow plugin already nicely supports schemas for response bodies, but it would be nice if it could also support schemas for request bodies/query params. I have a PR that could support this, but wanted to get feedback on the format. Here's what I was thinking:

### Body:
This would operate similarly to the existing response schema handling, where the schema would get replaced by a $ref if found in the definitions.
```yaml
post:
    description: Add your favorite pet
    parameters:
        - name: body
           schema: PetSchema
    responses:
        200:
            description: The pet that was added
            schema: PetSchema
```
### Query params:
This gets a little weird, since OpenAPI doesn't really support refs for query params AFAICT. What I was thinking is that we'd iterate through all the fields in the schema and make each one a parameter.
```yaml
get:
    description: Search by userId. Allows up to 100 driver ids
    parameters:
        - name: query
           schema: PetSchema
    responses:
        200:
            description: The pet that was added
            schema: PetSchema
```

issue_title: Confusion around the type for included data.
issue_body:
There seems to be a little confusion around the correct type for included data.

To quote the JSON API spec:
> In a compound document, all included resources **MUST** be represented as an array of resource objects in a top-level included member.

In [`marshmalllow_jsonapi.schema.Schema.__init__`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow-jsonapi/blob/dev/marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py#L96), the `included_data` member is initialised as a dict.

In [`_do_load`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow-jsonapi/blob/dev/marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py#L231), the `included_data` member defaults to a dict. However, it is iterated as a list inside [`_extract_from_included`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow-jsonapi/blob/dev/marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py#L259) which would be correct. This works if the payload is an array or undefined, because then `_extract_from_included` will either iterate the list or iterate the keys of an empty dict.

In [`render_included_data`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow-jsonapi/blob/dev/marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py#L133), the `included_data` member is expected to have a `values` method (i.e. dict-like).

The reason I think this has been working so far is because most people are not deserialising payloads with 'included' data. This is because the current spec has no provision for compound document requests (but there are some in draft), only responses.

The spec clearly states the the top-level included member is an array. It should be treated as a list everywhere.

The documentation on the timeout parameter is pretty clear:

```
timeout = x: set timeout to x seconds (float allowed) returns immediately 
when the requested number of bytes are available, otherwise wait until
the timeout expires and return all bytes that were received until then.
```

read_until however does never return if at least one byte is received within each timeout period. e.g. timeout=1 and the device sends 2bytes/second (but no terminator). This is definitely not the expected behavior, as a device sending unexpected data can block your application forever. 

The problem is caused by ``read(1)`` being called repeatedly without checking for the total time taken. https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/serial/serialutil.py#L640

MKL read environment variables to get configuration. These variables include MKL_DISABLE_FAST_MM, KMP_BLOCKTIME, OMP_WAIT_POLICY, OMP_NUM_THREADS. See https://github.com/intel-analytics/BigDL/blob/master/spark/dl/src/main/resources/spark-bigdl.conf.

omp.h
omp_set_num_threads, kmp_set_blocktime

issue_title: senza update does not correctly apply changes to ASG
issue_body:
I wanted to update the minimum and maximum of an ASG as well as the instance type with `senza update` for a dozen stacks. The instance type was updated successfully for all stacks. Unfortunately the min/max values of the ASGs have not been updated for most of the stacks. When I retry the update command for one of the stacks where it "failed" (there was never an error or something) the update is rejected with "No updates are to be performed."

Digging a bit deeper I found out that the CloudFormation templates have been updated for all stacks. So when I look at the output of `senza dump` or the CloudFormation designer in AWS' Management Console everything is correct. But the values have not been applied to the ASGs themselves. In my case I tried to update the min/max from 1/1 to 2/4. After the `senza update` for some stacks the ASGs in question still only had 1/1 whereas the CloudFormation template shows 2/4. I can see no difference between the stacks where it worked compared to those were it did not work.

I'm not sure whether this is a senza issue or not but this definitely impedes automation of certain tasks. For now I'll just fix it manually.

I recognize that there is not much tangible information in this issue but as everything went through without any error I don't know what more to say. If you can tell me ways to get more information, I'd be happy to provide it when the behavior happens again.

issue_title: Problems inheriting from gridData's Grid class
issue_body:
I was using Grid as a base class for a class (in cosmology). For now let's call this CosmoGrid. But I ran into some issues.

First, the arithmetic operations (add etc) return Grid. I am not a OOP ninja, but I think if you have them return self.__class__(...) instead, then they will return an instance of the same subclass  (CosmoGrid).

Second, one might want to wish to pass into Grid, not an ndarray but a subclass of an ndarray: for example an array of "Quantities" (that have astropy.units units attached, or a masked array). Will not the line self.grid = numpy.asarray(grid) convert this into a normal ndarray? (the docs say:  Returns out: ... If a is a subclass of ndarray, a *base class* ndarray is returned.) The alternative might be numpy.asanyarray, for which the docs say "Convert the input to an ndarray, but pass ndarray subclasses through."

I've been thinking for a while that maybe I should have named `Lambdify` differently for 2 reasons:

1. It's an esoteric name.
2. It makes you think that it is just a fast version of `sympy.lambdify`, which it is not since it does not support duck-typing (using arrays with units from e.g. `quantities` does the right thing when using `sympy.lambdify` but would drop the meta-data about units when used with `Lambdify`).

I think a name such as `Evalutator` or `NumEvaluator` or `FloatingPointEvaluator` (the last one's probably too long) would have been more appropriate. Any name change would of course keep `Lambdify` as a (deprecated) alias for the foreseeable future. What do you think?

在stock_account.py中的197行:_handle_dividend_book_closure函数中的通过dividend_reinvestment模拟股票分红再投资, 其代码如下:

```
    def _handle_dividend_book_closure(self, trading_date):
...
            if config.extra.dividend_reinvestment:
                last_price = Environment.get_instance().data_proxy.get_bar(order_book_id, trading_date).close
                shares = position.quantity * dividend_per_share / last_price
                position._quantity += shares
            else:
...

```
在上述代码中, 分红再投资是通过用分红当日的**收盘价**购买股票来模拟的, 这不太准确的。
若是要模拟得像未除权一样, 应该是 **用昨日的未复权收盘价卖出该支股票, 然后将卖得的所有资金再用今日的复权后昨收盘价购买该支股票**, 这样获得的股票份数就是模拟出的未除权份数.

如果是用追加购买的方式来模拟, 上面的代码应该是用**复权后**的**昨收盘价**进行购买, 而不应该用当日的收盘价购买。

The WebUI uses a Python logger to make its log. furthermore, the uWSGI also makes its own log.

As a result,we may have several log files:

 - [ ] webui-access.log, from uWSGI, that will contain the uWSGI HTTP requests
 - [ ] webui-error.log, from uWSGI, that will contain the WebUI console log
 - [ ] info.log, from WebUI, that will contain the INFO level log
 - [ ] warning.log, from WebUI, that will contain the WARNING level log
 - [ ] error.log, from WebUI, that will contain the ERROR level log

After some experiments, the 3 last configured files are not really interesting. Only both first files are pertinent. As such, I will make a default configuration with only the uWSGI log files activated.
